When joy reaches its peak, worry draws near;
Xun and Dui clearly show fortune and fear.
If not brought to light, all ends in gloom,
As the sun fades behind the western hills' loom.
interpretation1: This sign holds an opportunity for clarity, but glory is fleeting; maintain balance, guard against excess.
career: 'Setting sun's glow' indicates a brief rise or achievement in your career—like evening clouds, it may be transient. Seize the moment. 'Xun and Dui clearly show fortune and fear'—Dui involves disputes and gossip; Xun is gentle penetration. In the workplace, use soft power to avoid conflict. Dui transforming into Lü suggests roles in collaboration, service, or communication—especially public relations, education, arts. If promotion or project completion is near, avoid arrogance; tread cautiously and be humble. Favor: organizing procedures, refining details. Avoid: taking credit, overexpansion. Dui corresponds to autumn; opportunities come in autumn, but winter follows—prepare a fallback to prevent decline after peak.
wealth: 'Joy to worry' directly warns against greed in wealth—after a windfall, loss may follow. 'Setting sun's glow' hints at brief speculative opportunities like investments, side hustles, or unexpected gains—like a twilight glimmer, soon gone. Dui hexagram governs metal; wealth sources may come from metal, finance, or entertainment industries. But Lü hexagram's 'treading on a tiger's tail' warns against reckless speculation. Financial management should focus on preservation; allocate existing assets to stable channels. Avoid overextending credit or high leverage. Xun is wind—information-based profits need careful verification; don't follow rumors blindly. Fortune and fear are distinct: small gains are feasible, but avoid large pursuits; exit in time to be wise.
love: 'If not brought to light, all ends in gloom' suggests that if a relationship indulges in fleeting pleasure without nurturing roots, it tends to darken. Dui represents a young maiden and joy—love starts sweetly, but 'joy to worry' brings quarrels and doubt. Lü hexagram emphasizes responsibility and boundaries. For singles, this sign indicates ambiguous feelings that may not last; clarify intentions—express actively but not frivolously. For couples, avoid revisiting old grievances or forcing change; plan a future together with courtesy, so love can shine. 'Setting sun's glow' symbolizes rekindled passion for long-term partners—a chance to repair, but beware of deeper loneliness after the glow; daily care is the true path.
health: Dui represents mouth and lungs; Lü's upper trigram Qian is also Metal—fire and metal clash. 'Setting sun' hints at declining yang energy. Focus on respiratory system and lung care. Dui transforming into Lü requires careful steps; guard against joint or foot strain. 'Joy to worry' warns not to overexert despite energy—staying up late, feasting, and improper diet can easily cause deficient fire. Health regimen: sleep and rise early; eat white moistening foods like pear and white fungus. Exercise moderate, such as walking or Tai Chi; avoid profuse sweating. Xun is wind—guard against seasonal external pathogens during transitions. In autumn and winter, especially protect neck and back from drafts to prevent recurrence of old ailments.
study: 'If not brought to light, all ends in gloom' warns that shallow learning without deep thought will not bear fruit. Dui favors speech; Xun favors penetration—suitable for languages, literature, and arts; oral exams, interviews are favorable. 'Setting sun's glow' hints that last-minute cramming or targeted review can yield temporary results, but foundations are weak; exam scores may fluctuate—watch for carelessness causing point loss. Favor: regular study plans, avoiding last-minute cramming. Lü hexagram emphasizes practice—internships, competitions, and debates can illuminate your learning. Avoid studying in isolation. The favorable aspect is good comprehension; the unfavorable is restlessness and forgetfulness. Guard against pride and laziness to achieve consistency.
general: This sign's core is 'maintain abundance with caution.' When at peak, think of decline; when joyful, consider worry—only then can you avoid 'worry drawing near.' 'Xun and Dui clearly show fortune and fear' indicates that everything hangs on choice: gentleness and yielding bring fortune; rigidity and aggression bring fear. In action: first, draw in your horns—don't claim credit; attribute honors to the team or timing. Second, plan an exit early before the sun sets. Third, leave room in everything: don't speak fully, don't exhaust profits, don't drain emotions. In mindset, learn from Dui's joy with restraint and Lü's ritual caution. Let gloom be a mirror, reflecting the twilight glow—though brief, it warms the present. Nurture a modest light, and you may achieve radiance. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
Through waves three and five you have passed,
Now the waters calm, no wind at last.
See the azure path ahead so bright,
Advance or yield, each move brings light.
interpretation1: The opening line uses river waves to symbolize life's hardships. Now calm prevails, but hidden vigilance remains. The third line points to a bright path requiring clarity and timing. The hexagram shifts from Feng (abundance) to Xiao Guo (small excess), warning against overreaching. This is a neutral sign: maintain steadiness through awareness.
career: The waves suggest past turbulence in your career; the calm indicates a plateau. Avoid forcing progress now; instead, consolidate skills and await the right moment. For managers, delegate; for technical roles, refine expertise. Likely to advance when timing aligns.
wealth: Wealth has steadied after fluctuations. Stick to regular savings and low-risk investments. Favorable in water and earth-related industries. Avoid leverage or speculative moves. Small gains may come without aggressive effort.
love: Past emotional waves have settled. For singles, avoid rushing; nurture connections from existing circles. For couples, balance intimacy and space. Minor disputes may arise; address them gently. Relationships are likely to deepen with patience.
health: This hexagram relates to kidneys, bladder, and circulation. Past strain may linger, but current calm favors recovery. Adopt a light diet, reduce salt, and rest by 9–11 PM. Gentle movement like Qigong is advisable. Watch for tinnitus or lower back fatigue.
study: Past academic hurdles are easing. Use the calm to consolidate basics and avoid guesswork. Weak subjects may improve through steady review. Exam performance is likely solid with careful preparation. Avoid last-minute cramming.
general: This sign calls for balance after struggle. The key is to recognize the present calm as a preparation phase. Reflect on what you've learned from past waves, store energy, and act when the path becomes clear. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
The waning moon is full once more,
Withered branches fresh colors restore.
A broad and level road extends ahead,
Raise your eyes to the sky's blue spread.
interpretation1: The waxing moon symbolizes recovered opportunities; withered branches blooming anew indicate revival surpassing the past. The smooth road and upturned gaze promise clear progress and lofty vision. The hexagram Guimei (Returning Maiden) shifts to Lin (Overseeing), signifying harmony and rising fortune. This is supremely favorable: cherish the moment, act with gratitude.
career: Withered branches now bloom—past projects or stalled roles may revive and excel. Reunite with former collaborators or restart unfinished work. A promotion or recognition is likely. Favorable in communication, reconstruction, or cultural fields. Be mindful of pride; lead with humility.
wealth: Wealth may first dip then rise. Idle assets or old investments could yield returns. Stick to steady income; real estate and land-related fields are favorable. Avoid high-risk schemes. Gains may come from partnerships or unexpected gifts.
love: This hexagram strongly favors relationships: reunions, reconciliation, or renewed passion. Singles may reconnect with an old acquaintance; couples can deepen bonds through shared future planning. Avoid dwelling on past mistakes. Express affection openly.
health: Old ailments may resolve, and vitality can surpass previous levels. Guard against neck or head issues from prolonged upward gazing. Focus on lung and spleen health with gentle breathing exercises. Avoid overstrain during recovery.
study: Weak subjects or past exam disappointments may now turn into strengths. Pursue ambitious academic goals; language, literature, and philosophy are favored. Review old material to build a solid foundation. Exams are likely to go well.
general: This supremely favorable sign signals a turning point: what seemed lost is regained, and what seemed dead flourishes. The key actions are to revisit unfinished matters, walk steadily on the smooth road, and keep your gaze high. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Hard the road, hard the road,
Only now I know how hard the road.
Yet the vast future holds no fear,
With steadfast strength, pass the shoal here.
interpretation1: The repeated exclamation of hardship borrows from ancient verse, emphasizing real struggle leading to insight. The second half reverses the tone: the future is vast, the present obstacle is just a shoal. The hexagram shifts from Gen (mountain, stillness) to Jian (gradual progress), urging steady, patient effort. This is a supremely favorable sign: confidence born from difficulty, and the resolve to move forward.
career: Career bottlenecks are acknowledged; the insight gained is valuable. Do not force quick results; instead, refine processes and show reliability. Progress is likely in late spring. Fields like education, consulting, or those requiring endurance suit you. Avoid impulsive job changes.
wealth: Wealth requires experienced effort; easy money is unlikely. Focus on stable income, skill-based earnings, or rental returns. Investments should be gradual—regular savings and low-risk options. Avoid loans or speculation. Steady steps protect assets.
love: Relationships face difficulties but can deepen through honest communication. Singles may meet someone through cultural events or study; the connection is likely to grow slowly but solidly. For couples, a shared challenge can strengthen bonds. Avoid blame; address issues now.
health: This sign points to muscle or joint fatigue, or digestive issues. Gentle exercise like walking or Qigong is best. Diet should be warm and soft; avoid raw or heavy foods. Watch for knee weakness or bloating. Gradual care restores vitality.
study: Academic progress may feel arduous, but persistence leads to breakthroughs. A mentor or sudden insight could illuminate a topic. Consistent daily review is better than cramming. Exams favor the well-prepared; stay calm during tests.
general: This mixed fortune sign turns hardship into growth. The key is to accept the difficulty as part of the journey, trust the vast future, and apply steady effort today. After crossing the shoal, remain humble. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Spring scenery bright, spring hues fresh and new,
Spring feeling dwells where waters flow through.
Endless is the beauty of spring days,
Go feast in the jade forest rays.
interpretation1: Spring imagery unfolds renewal and opportunity. 'Spring by the water' hints at wisdom and wealth. The jade forest banquet symbolizes achievement and celebration. The hexagram shifts from Dun (retreat) to Lü (travel), signaling a transition from waiting to action. This is very favorable: seize the season, step forward to receive good news.
career: Career prospects brighten like spring. Water-related industries (trade, logistics, culture) are favored. A transfer, business trip, or public opportunity may arise. Past patience pays off; proactively showcase your abilities in competitions or meetings. Avoid hesitation.
wealth: Wealth flows like spring water. Positive short-term gains from career breakthroughs, banquets, or short-term trades. Activate idle funds; focus on liquid assets. Spring is ideal for signing contracts. Avoid long-term lock-ins or hoarding cash.
love: Romance blooms: singles should attend social gatherings, especially near water. A declaration or successful match is likely. Couples enjoy harmonious time; a short trip or celebration deepens connection. Be open and proactive, not withdrawn.
health: Vitality returns; any lingering gloom lifts. Liver and gallbladder health benefit from gentle expansion, while kidney and urinary systems should be supported with increased water intake and outdoor walks to assist the transition of seasonal qi. Guard against excessive heart fire (restlessness) and digestive upset. Go to bed early, rise early, and eat seasonal greens.
study: Studies enter a golden period. Subjects like literature, arts, and history flow with inspiration. Exam luck is strong for entrance exams or certifications. Join study groups and mock tests. Avoid isolation; collaborative discussion sharpens thinking.
general: This very favorable sign marks a season of action and celebration. The key is to move from retreat to engagement: attend gatherings, pursue new ventures, and share your achievements. Enjoy the spring, but remain grounded. Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment.

sign_text:
Ten thousand horses return to the source,
a thousand apes pay homage at the cave.
The tiger submits, the dragon bows—
high is the Way, deep the virtue.
interpretation1: The sign depicts a grand scene of all things returning to order and mighty forces yielding. 'Ten thousand horses return to the source': horses, once galloping in turmoil, now return to their origin, symbolizing the quieting of worldly strife. 'A thousand apes pay homage at the cave': apes, clever and restless, yet gather toward the cave, representing many cunning minds or scattered thoughts coming in—but their submission may not be genuine. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows': tigers and dragons, symbols of power, now lower their heads, indicating that strong opponents have temporarily sheathed their claws. Yet this sign is rated Neutral to Cautionary, advising inner cultivation rather than outward pursuit. The hexagram transforms from Da You (Great Possession) to Qian (The Creative). Da You is like the sun at zenith, full of abundance; transforming to Qian, the fiery light fades, leaving only the strong, pure heaven. This suggests that the peak of prosperity has passed; one should restrain brilliance and cultivate inner virtue. The sign points to 'high the Way, deep the virtue' as the key: to maintain harmony, profound moral character is essential; otherwise, submission may be illusory, and the crouching tiger may become a hidden menace. Overall meaning: temporary peace is not the end; true stability rests on deep virtue. A slight loss of balance easily brings relapse.
career: In career, 'ten thousand horses return to the source' suggests competition eases and projects settle, perhaps with integration or downsizing. But 'a thousand apes pay homage at the cave' warns that colleagues or subordinates may outwardly comply but harbor private agendas; the ape's cunning hints at hidden self-interest. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows' indicates that strong rivals or opponents temporarily yield, reducing pressure. At this time, follow 'high the Way, deep the virtue': consolidate authority through moral influence, e.g., proactively streamline processes, mediate differences, and demonstrate fairness. The timing is favorable around late autumn to early winter, suitable for closing-out tasks, audits, or team restructuring. Avoid: trusting verbal promises lightly, rushing into new rounds of expansion, or flaunting talent to stir old grievances. The Qian hexagram emphasizes strong action; from Da You to Qian, it is a time to rectify internal affairs and gather strength.
wealth: For wealth, 'ten thousand horses return to the source' means financial flows converge; earlier investments or debts may be recovered, but 'return to the source' also suggests profit margins narrow to normal. 'A thousand apes pay homage at the cave' shows external financing or cooperative offers seem abundant yet tricky to grasp, like elusive apes; careful screening is needed. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows' warns that financial dangers are latent; avoid rash moves. The hexagram turning from Da You to Qian shows abundant wealth dissipated, shifting to self-reliance. Thus wealth is not greatly favorable; focus on steady earning from work, such as salary or practical business, and avoid speculative windfalls. 'High the Way, deep the virtue' extends to managing finances with integrity, not chasing shortcuts. Specific advice: clear old accounts, build savings, suspend high-risk investments; consider fields related to land or traditional industries. Avoid: acting as a guarantor for others, blindly following partnerships.
love: In love, 'ten thousand horses return to the source' indicates emotional entanglements or choices have passed; relationships stabilize or return to plainness. 'A thousand apes pay homage at the cave' suggests lingering temptations or restless thoughts within; singles may receive multiple advances but feel unsettled. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows' means old conflicts have temporarily subsided, but if virtue is insufficient, resentment may build. The Neutral to Cautionary sign reminds you that 'high the Way, deep the virtue' is key: emotional maintenance relies on honesty and tolerance. Treat your partner with generosity, not by forcing submission. Singles should engage in self-reflection and cultivate heart, avoiding hasty choices. Couples should share family responsibilities and revisit initial promises; avoid dredging up past grievances or arguing fiercely. Qian hexagram advocates self-strengthening; similarly, in love, cultivate your own virtue to attract sincerity.
health: Regarding health, 'ten thousand horses return to the source' corresponds to qi and blood calming from agitation to rest, suggesting a need to shift from exertion to recuperation. Beware of excessive heart fire causing insomnia or headaches. 'A thousand apes pay homage at the cave' uses apes as a metaphor for troubled thoughts; guard against anxiety harming the spleen, leading to digestive issues and fluctuating appetite. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows'—the tiger represents liver qi, the dragon represents yang qi; improper submission may cause liver depression or yang hyperactivity, hiding risks of dizziness or blood pressure fluctuations. The transformation from Da You to Qian, fire fading to heaven, warns against imbalance between heart fire and kidney water. The way of health lies in 'high the Way, deep the virtue'—maintain regular routines, a tranquil mind; rise gently in the morning, sleep peacefully at night; eat spleen-nourishing foods like millet and yam; avoid spicy or agitating items. Watch for signs: unexplained irritability, vivid dreams, stiff neck and shoulders.
study: In studies, 'ten thousand horses return to the source' indicates that the phase of wide-ranging learning is over; you need to return to basics, focusing on core subjects or foundational concepts. 'Return to the source' means consolidating fundamentals. 'A thousand apes pay homage at the cave' warns against a restless mind; you may be distracted by external influences or try to cover too much, leading to superficial understanding. Discipline yourself to tame restlessness. 'The tiger submits, the dragon bows' suggests that difficult chapters or strong competitors no longer pose obstacles, but complacency may cause fluctuating results. The sign's 'high the Way, deep the virtue' implies that learning requires reverence; accumulated virtue (solid accumulation) leads to distance. Exam luck is moderate; favor essay and comprehensive questions. Practice classic problems repeatedly. Avoid: cutting corners or rote memorization. Qian hexagram advocates constant movement; daily lessons cannot be neglected; morning recitation is especially beneficial for memory.
general: Overall, Hexagram 37 presents an image of superficial submission and inner potential change. All things returning to order is not natural; it relies entirely on 'high the Way, deep the virtue'. If virtue wavers, the 'tiger submits' may become a hidden hazard, and 'apes pay homage' may turn into disaffection. The current situation requires grasping three points: first, advance by retreating—secure the foundation, do not be fooled by apparent harmony into rushing forward; second, cultivate virtue and reflect—treat others with fairness and generosity to dissolve hidden suspicions; third, sheathe your brilliance—since Da You has turned to Qian, act with the self-renewing force of heaven but avoid ostentation. Mentally regard 'return to the source' as an opportunity for settling, not as a grand accomplishment. Hold humility and reverence; thus you may transform moderate unfavorable into stability. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
The oriole announces the imperial grove,
spring colors bright and clear.
Lift the whip quickly, spur the horse on—
speed your journey.
interpretation1: This neutral sign indicates that the advantage still exists; the danger lies in delay leading to change.
career: The line 'the oriole announces the imperial grove' suggests good news in your career—perhaps an internal promotion, an external offer, or a major project launch. 'Spring colors bright and clear' means the situation is clear and direction is set. 'Lift the whip quickly, spur the horse on' urges immediate action. This sign's career trend is like a horse ready to gallop; seize opportunities in the first half (spring or now). Xun hexagram favors roles requiring communication and coordination; but Huan hexagram hints at team dispersion or direction changes, so steady your core goals amid transitions. Specific advice: proactively volunteer for pioneering tasks, assignments, or roles that require quick decisions. Avoid: hesitation, waiting for opportunities, or getting distracted by multiple options and easily changing plans. If facing a choice, pick the main path and advance quickly; do not deviate for novelty.
wealth: 'Spring colors bright and clear' is beautiful, but wealth, like spring light, is fleeting. 'Lift the whips quickly, spur the horse on' emphasizes speed. The imagery points to short-term visible gains, such as quick returns from regular income or short-term speculative opportunities. The Xun hexagram has a saying of 'near profit threefold', but the change to Huan suggests money disperses easily; so after acquiring money, move to stable channels—do not repeatedly add to high-risk investments. Specific sources: fields related to Xun Wood, such as culture, greenery, trade; also small, fast-moving investments. Financial attitude: be decisive and timely. Avoid: dragging out decisions, or spreading too thin causing fund dispersion. For regular income, consider asking for a raise or performance bonus now; do not miss out due to modesty.
love: The oriole's spring song brings lively news to the emotional world. Singles may suddenly meet someone, like first sight in the imperial grove; express your feelings immediately, do not let the flower fall from the branch. 'Lift the whip quickly' here means proactive invitation and direct communication, not reserved waiting. For couples, the Huan hexagram warns of distance from daily trivialities or external interference; the sign suggests planning a short trip or new project together, using 'speed your journey' to renew freshness. If in ambiguity, 'spring colors bright and clear' signals that the veil is about to lift; advance honestly, do not blur lines. In relationships, create shared forward-moving goals; avoid revisiting old grievances or letting the relationship stagnate. If it has become distant, use the yielding Xun wind to dissolve dispersion with gentle communication.
health: The oriole singing in spring corresponds to rising liver qi. The imagery indicates overall health improving with the season, but 'lift the whip quickly' hints at rushing and toil, draining qi and blood. Xun is wind; guard against wind pathogens, especially during season changes, paying attention to respiratory and joint issues. The changing hexagram Huan is water, suggesting kidney qi or urinary system may be prone to dispersion; appropriately strengthen kidneys and avoid overwork. For health maintenance, take gentle morning walks like a horse treading on spring grass to absorb spring energy. Diet: green vegetables nourish the liver, supplemented with black beans and walnuts to firm kidneys. Warning signs: if you suddenly feel tired or unfocused, reduce your load and rest; do not rely on 'bright spring colors' to overdraw your stamina.
study: 'Spring colors bright and clear' signifies sudden clarity in studies; previously obscure points become clear, especially beneficial for liberal arts, arts, or subjects requiring inspiration. The oriole's pleasing song favors language and music learning. But 'speed your journey' reveals this is a sprint, not a marathon: exams or important milestones are approaching; concentrate your efforts like a knight whipping his horse. Xun hexagram favors penetration; Huan hexagram warns against spreading too many subjects simultaneously. Make a compact plan to quickly clear knowledge gaps. Avoid: procrastination or chasing perfection at the expense of progress. In exams, first secure points you are sure of; gallop ahead, don't linger too long on one question and scatter your mind.
general: Overall, current fortune is like spring dawn, with a trending clarity; the outcome of this neutral sign hinges on the word 'speed'; slow leads to dispersion.

sign_text:
Broadly dispense the wondrous elixir,
save thousands of people.
At last reach the other shore,
cross over and join the ranks of immortals.
interpretation1: The sign opens with 'Broadly dispense the wondrous elixir, save thousands of people'—a grand vision: the elixir symbolizes precious resources, generously bestowed without selfishness; saving thousands echoes Yi hexagram's nurturing virtue, and Sun hexagram points that this process involves personal loss, yet that is precisely where merit lies. 'At last reach the other shore, cross over and join the ranks of immortals'—the other shore and immortal ranks symbolize transcendence from worldly toil. The four lines flow together, revealing a causal chain of gaining through giving. Xun is wind, penetrating and gradual; Yi hexagram nourishes rectitude; Sun hexagram 'diminishes the lower to benefit the upper.' Combined, the sign's core is 'nurture by giving'—seeming dispersion actually accumulates depth. The hexagram change from Yi to Sun is the turning point from internal cultivation to external manifestation: giving is not dissipation but transformation into higher blessings. Overall meaning: goodness is never lost; benevolent acts will eventually find a ferry. But maintain persistence and rightness; do not doubt because of delayed return.
career: For career, explore 'dispense the wondrous elixir' and 'reach the other shore.' 'Dispense the wondrous elixir' means generously sharing experience, resources, or mentoring junior colleagues; 'save thousands' implies your efforts can unite the team and build a broad network. The Sun hexagram suggests short-term credit may be diluted, but Xun wind gradually penetrates, eventually building reputation. 'Reach the other shore' indicates career breakthroughs, like project approval or promotion, especially beneficial for consulting, education, medicine and other 'teaching' industries. Favorable actions: actively participate in training, cross-department collaboration; transform personal results into shared assets. Avoid: hoarding credit, hesitating to help, or rushing for results. Timing: changes likely around autumn-winter; before that, silently cultivate skills like refining the elixir.
wealth: Wealth in heaven and earth circulates; hoarding money is like hoarding medicine—only by giving to others can you restore yourself.
love: 'Wondrous elixir' in love represents genuine care and affection; 'broadly dispense' is active giving without expectation of return. 'Save thousands' alludes to tolerance and conflict resolution in relationships. 'Reach the other shore' symbolizes going through the adjustment period to reach harmony, even a successful union. Sun hexagram reminds that love requires reducing self-centeredness, prioritizing the other's needs. Singles: this sign favors 'give love to receive love'; start by offering silent support and sincere help rather than directly pursuing. Couples: the nurturing imagery suggests tending the relationship through daily acts—sharing burdens, creating surprises. Avoid: weighing who gives more, or rehashing old grudges that harm trust. 'Cross over and join the immortals' is not distant but found in mutual fulfillment.
health: 'Wondrous elixir' here refers to essence-qi-shen; 'broadly dispense' warns against excessive depletion. 'Save thousands' may easily lead to unaware fatigue. Yi hexagram originally nourishes, but the change to Sun indicates that health must know when to stop; otherwise, it harms the foundation. 'Reach the other shore' means proper adjustment can cross the river of sub-health; 'join the immortals' symbolizes returning to natural peace. Watch for signs: Xun wind corresponds to liver-gallbladder and nerves; Yi-Sun change especially requires caring for spleen-stomach and avoiding worry. Suitable practices: regular meals at 70% fullness; gentle morning walks like wind through willow; afternoon naps to nourish yin. Avoid: staying up late, overeating, or emotional highs and lows. Consider rest as another form of 'dispensing elixir'—recharge vitality through stillness.
study: On the path of learning, 'wondrous elixir' symbolizes refined knowledge or effective methods; 'broadly dispense' means enjoying exchange and discussion; 'save thousands' indicates teaching benefits teacher and student alike; Sun hexagram shows that output actually solidifies one's own learning. 'Reach the other shore' corresponds to smooth transition at key exams or advancement. Xun wind favors thorough thinking; Yi hexagram emphasizes accumulation, so study should involve deep immersion, not fragmented browsing. Beneficial for liberal arts, classics, or subjects requiring patient grounding. Exam luck: early formation of study groups for mutual teaching and sharing notes and diagrams; in exams, you may draw analogies easily. Avoid: studying alone stubbornly, hoarding materials, or relying on quick-fix 'elixirs.' Effort lies in daily practice; crossing the river requires your own pole.
general: This sign uses 'shedding the boat to reach shore' as an overall metaphor: the broader your heart, the deeper your blessings. Giving may seem like a loss, but it plants good seeds that eventually yield good fruit. Key action suggestions: First, perform one anonymous act of kindness each month, like sharing resources anonymously or guiding the lost. Second, set up an 'altruistic fund' of time or small money, not expecting immediate return. Third, when encountering difficulties, meditate that 'saving others is saving yourself'; shift anxiety by helping others. Mentally, avoid short-termism; dispense the elixir with calm, trusting that good deeds never fade. Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
A belt of water, clear and deep.
The boat stays on the river, the moon at the center of the sky.
Steady steps within, subtle and not idle.
It is not others who mislead you; you have almost lost your very life.
interpretation1: The first line 'A belt of water, clear and deep' uses the image of clear river water that hides deep currents—a metaphor for a situation that appears calm on the surface but conceals subtle dangers. The next line 'The boat stays on the river, the moon at the center of the sky': the boat is moored, not advancing; the moon is at its zenith, an extreme moment hinting at turning to decline, suggesting stagnation and impending reversal. 'Steady steps within, subtle and not idle' points out that if you merely tread carefully without adapting, the subtle mysteries will not grant ease, and you will be burdened by worldly affairs. The closing line 'It is not others who mislead you; almost lost your very life' is the sign's eye—directly stating that it is not external people but your own attachment or overconfidence that nearly caused great harm. The hexagram belongs to the Qian Palace; the original hexagram is Guan (Contemplation), changing to Huan (Dispersion). Guan implies wind on the earth, signifying examination; Huan is wind over water, dispersion. Moving from observation to dispersal warns that if you are overconfident in what you see and feel, not knowing when to advance or retreat, you may disperse and harm yourself. Core meaning: external calm, inner danger; self-deception is the greatest risk. Stay still, perceive change, examine yourself carefully; do not cling to your own view, lest you mislead yourself.
career: 'The boat stays on the river, the moon at the center of the sky' symbolizes career stagnation at a seemingly perfect point; the bright moon at its zenith will soon set, warning of a turning point from peak to decline. The hexagram change from Guan to Huan—Guan for examination, Huan for dissipation—suggests that in the workplace, overconfidence or sticking to old methods may lead to team dispersal or project deviation. Favorable actions: re-evaluate your position; like 'a belt of water' clear self-reflection, actively seek change to avoid rigidity. Suitable for internal coordination or process optimization roles, not outward expansion. Timing: around autumn-winter, fluctuations may occur; watch for interpersonal conflicts. Avoid: stubbornness, ignoring colleagues' advice; otherwise 'it is not others who mislead you' and self-will may cause missed opportunities or even career harm.
wealth: The water 'clear and deep' seems transparent, yet 'subtle and not idle' indicates wealth appears stable but is actually unreliable and elusive. 'The boat stays on the river' suggests wealth movement is sluggish; do not force it. Huan hexagram indicates money easily disperses and is hard to gather; investments risk being lost to disintegration. This wealth leans toward regular income; solidify your main job, avoid speculation or partnerships. 'The moon at the center of the sky' is a sign of fullness, but fullness invites loss; it's wise to close out early. Specific advice: cut expenses, adopt conservative financial management, especially avoid expanding water-related industries (like logistics, tourism). Consider earth-related industries (land, construction) for stability. Avoid: trusting sweet words of others to join arrangements; otherwise 'almost lost your very life'—there may be risk of major financial loss.
love: 'The boat stays on the river' suggests a stalled relationship like a boat anchored midstream, unable to advance. 'The moon at the center of the sky' indicates emotions have peaked but may cool, gradually drifting apart. The hexagram change from Guan to Huan: over-observation breeds suspicion, leading to dispersal. Singles: do not force things; encounters now are more like illusions. 'Subtle and not idle' suggests self-limitation or overly high standards cause missing good matches. Couples: guard against 'it is not others who mislead you'—do not project your own suspicions onto your partner. Instead, communicate calmly; avoid revisiting grievances or cold-shouldering; otherwise 'almost lost your very life'—the relationship may be on the verge of collapse. The way forward: like 'steady steps within,' proceed cautiously, with mutual empathy, to cross these dangerous currents.
health: 'A belt of water' images the kidneys and urinary system; clear but with hidden currents indicates surface absence of pain but potential latent illness. 'The moon at the center of the sky' marks the time when yang expires and yin arises, warning of depletion from irregular routines or overwork. Huan hexagram means qi and blood easily disperse but hard to gather; guard against mental distraction, insomnia, anxiety. 'Subtle and not idle' further indicates the mind unable to find peace—a major health risk. Suitable practices: regulate diet, eat more black foods to nourish kidneys; gentle morning walks to stretch; avoid staying up late pushing yourself. Health direction focuses on stillness and rest. 'It is not others who mislead you' warns that health problems mostly stem from self-neglect; schedule regular check-ups, don't hide illness for fear of treatment.
study: 'The boat stays on the river' represents being stuck in a learning bottleneck, like a boat unable to move. 'The moon at the center of the sky' indicates studying late into the night but inefficiently. The hexagram change from Guan to Huan suggests observation is scattered, hard to focus, a state of dissipation. In studies, self-reflect on methods; avoid rote learning. 'Subtle and not idle' advises flexibility, not mechanical repetition. Exam luck: risk of underperformance, especially in subjects requiring on-the-spot adaptability. 'It is not others who mislead you' emphasizes not blaming external factors—examine your own knowledge gaps. Favorable actions: return to basics, review old material to gain new insights; like 'steady steps within,' move with solid steps. Avoid: trying to chew too much, or relying on quick-fix tricks.
general: The core of this sign is 'it is not others who mislead you'—all difficulties arise from your own attachment or self-satisfaction; the external world merely mirrors this. Guan hexagram offers an opportunity for examination; Huan hexagram warns of dispersal. It advises humility and introspection, don't wait until 'almost lost your very life' to regret. Two practical suggestions: First, like 'the moon at the center of the sky,' when at the peak, prepare for decline; always leave room and stop while ahead. Second, like 'the boat stays on the river,' a temporary halt may be preservation; do not force your way through dangerous rapids. Mentally, understand the clarity of 'clear and deep' water; let go of ego to perceive true signs and turn danger into safety. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
Peach and plum trees bloom in splendor,
Spring light is fresh and bright.
Remember this fine time and beautiful scene,
Follow your heart, all matters will fit.
Do not suspect vainly and disturb the gods.
interpretation1:
The sign text 'Peach and plum trees bloom in splendor, spring light is fresh and bright' uses the image of competing blossoms and bright spring to symbolize smooth fortunes and favorable circumstances. Peach and plum are fine trees, representing gathering talents and abundant opportunities; 'spring light fresh and bright' speaks of a beautiful external environment, extending to harmonious conditions and smooth affairs. 'Remember this fine time and beautiful scene' warns not to waste the good times, but to cherish present opportunities; 'follow your heart, all matters will fit' does not mean indulgence, but rather to act in accordance with the natural flow and remain true to oneself, then everything will be at ease. The closing line 'Do not suspect vainly and disturb the gods' hits at a common human flaw: when things go well, one may become suspicious and thus lose heavenly harmony.

The hexagram belongs to Kan Palace; Kan is water, symbolizing danger and pitfalls, but the changing hexagram is Water-Fire Ji Ji (After Completion), indicating that the initial difficulty is passing and achievements are about to be made—safety within danger. The judgment of Ji Ji says 'initial good fortune, final disorder,' which fits the meaning of a Mixed sign: although the spring light is beautiful now, there is a concern that prosperity is fleeting and extreme prosperity will lead to decline. Therefore, the key to interpreting this sign is to know the time, cherish blessings, act with moderation and uprightness, and not to become arrogant or lazy due to smoothness, nor to lose composure due to suspicion. Overall meaning: seize the present moment, trust that Heaven rewards diligence, and do not ruin good opportunities by petty doubts.
career:
The sign text 'Peach and plum trees bloom' implies that your career is in a period of growth, with opportunities like spring flowers. It is especially favorable for fields that require showcasing talent, such as culture, education, art, and public relations. 'Follow your heart' indicates that now is the time to act according to your nature, not to force a direction, but to focus on depth. The hexagram Kan Palace, Zhun transforming into Ji Ji: initially there may be obstacles, but the changing hexagram Ji Ji indicates eventual small success. Therefore, career bottlenecks may be resolved, and previous difficulties can be alleviated. Specifically, matters such as signing contracts, reporting, and bidding are more likely to succeed if advanced around the spring months, benefiting from 'fine time and beautiful scene'.

Recommended: Actively showcase your abilities; take on projects that allow creative expression; cooperate with others and leverage team strength to achieve goals. Avoid: Suspecting or being jealous of colleagues; frequent hesitation and indecision; neglecting details due to small successes, leading to regret over 'initial good fortune, final disorder.' Kan water represents wisdom, and Ji Ji hexagram has water above fire, indicating that communication is paramount; avoid emotional conflicts.
wealth:
Peach and plum in full garden symbolize wealth with roots—not sudden fortune, but steady income through skills and knowledge. 'Spring light fresh and bright' indicates that financial luck is in season, with positive regular income expected, especially in spring-related industries. However, Kan Palace water is unpredictable, and Ji Ji hexagram warns against overexpansion, so speculative gains are not advisable. 'Do not suspect vainly' is a key caution: if you hesitate when opportunities arise, you may miss them; but if you are overly suspicious and make rash changes, you may also harm your finances.

Recommended: Focus on stable income; advance your main profession; make small savings to prepare for future needs; consider water-related or mobile industries (e.g., logistics, trade). Avoid: Blindly following investment trends; borrowing for expansion; arguing over small profits that could damage interpersonal wealth channels. The way of managing finances is like peach and plum trees: root deeply, and naturally you will harvest abundance.
love:
'Peach and plum trees bloom' directly points to romantic spring. Single individuals may encounter a good match; romantic opportunities are abundant but not frivolous, and the encounter may be as dignified and beautiful as spring flowers. 'Remember this fine time and beautiful scene' advises you to cherish the person before you. For those already in a relationship, this period is harmonious and sweet, so it is a good time to create romantic moments and rekindle initial feelings. Kan Palace implies that the path of love may not be entirely smooth; Ji Ji hexagram shows water and fire interacting, suggesting the need to balance giving and receiving.

For singles: Participate in refined gatherings or cultural trips; interact naturally to attract like-minded people; avoid forcing a relationship or doubting your own charm. For couples: Actively express affection, create surprises, and revisit early memories; avoid suspicion over trivial matters, dwelling on old grievances, or using emotional coldness. 'Do not suspect vainly' is particularly important in love: trust is the foundation; once doubt arises, it is like a spring chill that damages the roots.
health:
Spring, though beautiful, may affect the liver wood element. Peach and plum belong to wood, corresponding to the liver; the sign word 'bloom' suggests a need for smooth flow and regulation. In traditional Chinese medicine, Kan Palace is associated with kidney water, and Ji Ji hexagram indicates water above fire, suggesting the need for balance between yin and yang. Currently, health trends are stable, but because this is a Mixed sign with the potential of initial danger (Zhun), one should not overindulge due to temporary well-being.

Recommended: Morning walks and breathing exercises to promote the rising spring qi; eat fresh vegetables and sprouts, reduce sour and increase sweet to nourish the spleen; go to bed by the Hai hour (9-11 PM) to nourish yin. Be mindful of: overthinking leading to insomnia and dry mouth; spring lethargy causing laziness in exercise; Kan water corresponds to ears, so pay attention to hearing and minor urinary issues. Remember that 'fine time and beautiful scene' require a strong body to enjoy; do not squander health due to youth.
study:
Peach and plum in full garden are signs of academic achievement; 'spring light fresh and bright' further indicates meeting good teachers and friends in a bright environment. 'Follow your heart' encourages studying from interest rather than rote memorization, leading to faster comprehension. Kan Palace represents wisdom; Ji Ji hexagram combines light and shadow, favoring deep research and analogical thinking. For exam takers, this sign is especially favorable for liberal arts, arts, and traditional culture; exam time will bring smooth thinking like spring water.

Recommended: Create a review plan that reinforces old knowledge while learning new; use spring mornings for recitation; discuss with classmates to complement each other; before exams, visit the exam venue and adjust your schedule. Avoid: Doubting your own ability; frequently changing study methods; trying to cover too much without focus. 'Do not suspect vainly and disturb the gods' implies not to deny yourself due to fluctuations in mock exams; with sincerity and determination, natural support will come.
general:
Sign 41 centers on 'spring light,' urging you to seize the moment and follow the flow. However, the hexagram warns of hidden dangers (Kan) and eventual decline (Ji Ji's 'final disorder'), so maintaining moderation is essential. Three key action points: First, grasp present opportunities—as the sign says, 'follow your heart,' act with the spring season, do not hesitate. Second, act with measure—enjoy the smoothness of 'all matters fitting,' but remember that 'fine time is fleeting,' so leave room for retreat; do not squander heavenly gifts. Third, cultivate the mind—'do not suspect vainly' reduces inner friction; avoid self-doubt, suspicion of others, or distrust of heaven; with clarity and openness, blessings will grow. In mindset, remember that spring radiance does not last; pick flowers while they bloom, but also nurture the roots to withstand storms.

Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
Hidden yet manifest, manifest yet subtle,
Within lies the secret key.
If you penetrate it thoroughly,
You may ascend the immortal ladder directly.
interpretation1: The first line 'Hidden yet manifest' signifies that concealed opportunities are gradually becoming clear, like morning mist dispersing to reveal the true form; 'manifest yet subtle' indicates that even within clarity there remains fine intricacy, so one must not stop at the surface. 'Within lies the secret key' points to the core of this sign: beneath the surface phenomena lies the critical thread that determines success or failure. 'If you penetrate it thoroughly, you may ascend the immortal ladder directly' is a commendation: if you can see through the mystery, you can make a leap in attainment and transcend to a higher level. The hexagram Gu (Decay) from Xun Palace, transforming into Da Chu (Great Accumulation). Gu (Decay) is like decay breeding worms, requiring rectification and renewal; Da Chu represents great accumulation, nourishing virtue and talent. The connection between the two matches 'hidden yet manifest'—when decay is exposed, the opportunity for renewal becomes manifest; when renewal is achieved, virtue and ability are greatly accumulated. This is a pattern of breaking down and then establishing, difficulty leading to smooth passage. Overall meaning: The current situation may appear stable or latent, but the turning point has arrived. Success or failure depends on whether you can penetrate the fog and grasp that one 'secret key.' As a supremely favorable sign, it does not grant blessings freely but rewards insight and comprehension. Cherish this moment, observe the subtlety of people and events, and do not be complacent due to apparent optimism.
career: 'Hidden yet manifest' corresponds to undercurrents in your career—such as team issues, process loopholes, or implicit expectations from superiors—which are gradually surfacing. This is precisely an opportunity to make contributions. 'Manifest yet subtle' reminds you that behind obvious task targets may lie deeper power dynamics or faction considerations. Gu (Decay) hexagram focuses on rectifying accumulated problems; it is advisable to proactively review old cases, optimize workflows, and clear up lingering issues. This is especially favorable for roles in auditing, inspection, R&D, and project management. 'If you penetrate it thoroughly' means that if you can grasp the real pain points of the organization and propose targeted solutions, you may receive extraordinary promotions or be entrusted with core projects, akin to Da Chu's 'advantageous to cross great rivers.' The timing is when contradictions concentrate; boldly advise superiors, and avoid following the crowd or shying away from difficulties. Avoid: Doing superficial work, believing in surface optimism, ignoring interpersonal undercurrents.
wealth: 'Hidden yet manifest' indicates that wealth luck is turning from dark to light—previously overlooked income sources or hidden liabilities will emerge. 'Manifest yet subtle' warns that within apparently clear financial opportunities lie detailed traps, such as contract terms and partner backgrounds that need careful examination. Gu hexagram symbolizes decay, suggesting financial accumulation problems to be cleared—bad debts, slow-moving inventory—which may yield unexpected recovery after cleanup. Da Chu hexagram favors accumulation and long-term investment. 'If you penetrate it thoroughly' means that if you can see through the subtlety of a business model or investment logic, you may make extraordinary returns. Wealth sources lean toward earned income; speculative gains require professional insight, such as discovering undervalued assets or technical patents. Recommended: Re-examine financial statements; invest in traditional industries (Xun wind represents penetration) or stable fields like warehousing and real estate (Gen mountain in Da Chu). Avoid: Blindly following trends, believing in high-interest promises.
love: 'Hidden yet manifest' in love refers to ambiguous relationships becoming clear, or hidden feelings or dissatisfaction in a partner gradually surfacing. 'Manifest yet subtle' means that even after confession, the other person's reaction or the relationship's direction still has subtle variables that require careful observation. Gu hexagram calls for rectification—if there are accumulated issues in the relationship (trust crises, family resistance), now is the time for honest communication and targeted resolution. Da Chu hexagram emphasizes containment; stable relationships should accumulate shared goals. For singles, 'hidden yet manifest' may indicate that a familiar acquaintance is showing special affection; 'the secret key' may imply an easily overlooked quality, and penetrating it could elevate the relationship. For those in relationships, focus on unspoken emotional needs, avoid negligence or revisiting old grievances. For married couples, it is favorable for resolving long-standing heart knots; if you can penetrate the root of family conflicts, the relationship may reach a new level.
health: Gu hexagram depicts wind entering a mountain, indicating blocked qi flow, requiring attention to liver and gallbladder (Xun wind, wood element) or spleen and stomach (Gen mountain, earth) stagnation. 'Hidden yet manifest' suggests that latent sub-health issues will show signs, such as chronic fatigue or hidden pains—an opportunity for regulation. 'Manifest yet subtle' means that apparent symptoms may have deeper imbalances in qi and blood; do not treat only the symptoms. Da Chu hexagram emphasizes accumulation; health preservation should focus on storing essence and avoiding depletion. 'If you penetrate it thoroughly' implies that if you can understand the root of your body's warning signals, you may regain health. Recommended: Morning qigong or stretches to soothe the liver; diet with more sweet and less sour; avoid overeating and staying up late that damage the liver. Be mindful of signs: rib pain, bloating, unexplained irritability.
study: 'Hidden yet manifest' corresponds to academic bottlenecks or confusion about to be clarified; previously difficult concepts will become clear. 'Manifest yet subtle' reminds you that beneath the surface formulas and theorems, you need to delve into their principles to achieve mastery. Gu hexagram symbolizes outdated knowledge that needs renewal—review errors and correct methods. Da Chu hexagram favors extensive reading and accumulation. 'If you penetrate it thoroughly' means that if you can grasp the inner logic of a subject or the trap setting of exam questions, you may make a leap in ranking. Exam luck favors deep understanding; sciences require attention to implicit conditions; liberal arts benefit from analyzing subtle meanings. Recommended: Tackle comprehensive problems; participate in discussions to spark insight. Avoid: Rote memorization, superficial learning.
general:
The essence of this sign is 'see the subtle and know the significant, break the cocoon and ascend the ladder.' In your current situation, beneath the surface lies a mysterious arrangement, with ladders of ascent hidden everywhere. Three key actions: First, stop macro planning and instead examine the abnormal details in people, events, and objects—the breakthrough lies there. Second, proactively clear old accounts, accumulated problems, or unfinished tasks; the environment will become clear. Third, remain humble even when the situation seems bright; keep asking 'is there still unseen subtlety?' In mindset, this supremely favorable sign is auspicious, but the condition 'penetrate thoroughly' must be met. Do not become proud or lazy; verify your insights step by step with cautious optimism. The virtue of Da Chu is accumulation; do not seek quick success. When the mystery reveals itself, blessings will come naturally.

Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
No going up, it is ahead,
Turn back and realize—collect the reins well.
A thousand paths and ten thousand roads always exist,
Seek them out yourself.
interpretation1: This sign uses a horse as a metaphor. The core image is 'No going up, it is ahead'—it may seem there is another peak to climb, but the sign advises you not to blindly pursue the summit. 'Turn back and realize, collect the reins well' is the key point: it is wise to pull in the reins in time and rein in your mind. The last two lines 'A thousand paths and ten thousand roads always exist, seek them yourself' comfort the seeker: the world is vast, and there is never only one way out; the key is to search with a calm mind. The hexagram belongs to Kun Palace, Da Zhuang (Great Strength) transforming into Tai (Peace). Da Zhuang depicts thunder moving across the sky, fierce momentum; but it changes to Earth-Heaven Tai, which represents a blend of strength and gentleness, tranquility and harmony—corresponding to the sign's call to 'collect the reins': shift from aggression to compliant conduct to avoid danger and attain good fortune. Kun Palace emphasizes softness and receptivity, reinforcing the theme of restraining one's edge and adapting to the times. This sign is particularly a warning for those with strong attachments and drive, teaching them to know when to advance and when to retreat. 'Turning back' is not defeat but a different path.
career: The 'horse' in the sign symbolizes career advancement; 'the reins' represent control over direction and pace. 'No going up' directly indicates that now is not the time to push for promotion or aggressive expansion, as there may be excessive competition or unnecessary conflict. Da Zhuang transforming into Tai shows that previous vigorous actions should now become gentle and steady; it is advisable to tone down your edge, review internal affairs, or adjust your track. Specifically, slow down your pace, look back at past oversights, and focus on consolidating existing results and optimizing team collaboration. If stuck, consider a horizontal move, job rotation, or exploring a different path among the 'thousand roads.' Avoid: Stubbornness, confrontation with superiors, blind expansion of new projects. This sign favors industries requiring inward cultivation, such as education, consulting, and culture.
wealth: The key to wealth is 'collect the reins well,' clearly indicating that it is better to hold than to attack. 'Thousand paths and ten thousand roads' show that income sources are not limited, but you must 'turn back and realize' before seeking; do not force it. Da Zhuang transforming into Tai: initially there may be seemingly lucrative high-risk investment opportunities, but they are likely to damage your foundation due to excessive aggression. The change to Tai suggests returning to stability for small gains. Kun Palace belongs to earth, relating to real estate, warehousing, agriculture—if opportunities arise, seek stable income from these areas. Avoid: Borrowing for expansion, chasing short-term speculation, taking the lead in partnerships. Recommended: Review your financial structure, reduce unnecessary expenses, adopt conservative investments; for windfall gains, focus on low-risk long-term vehicles. 'Seek them yourself' emphasizes personal research; do not trust others blindly.
love: 'Turn back and realize' is the eye of this sign for love. Singles may be stuck in an obsession or excessive expectations for a particular person; the sign advises turning around—perhaps there is a neglected affection nearby, or someone from the past may be worth re-evaluating. 'Collect the reins well' warns against chasing too hard; rein in excessive emotions to avoid self-harm. For those in relationships, this sign reminds you not to demand that your partner change according to your wishes; when disagreements arise, 'collect the reins' and retreat, then reflect on your own words and attitude. Da Zhuang transforming into Tai: from intensity to gentleness is a sign of warming feelings; use softness to overcome hardness. 'Thousand paths' means that if this road does not work, there are always others; do not fixate on one relationship.
health: 'Turn back and realize' corresponds to reviewing unhealthy habits in your life; 'reins' metaphorically represent bodily restraint. Da Zhuang hexagram suggests overexertion—if you have long been overworking, staying up late, or eating irregularly, now is the time to 'collect the reins' and recuperate. Kun Palace governs the spleen and stomach; Tai hexagram emphasizes balance. Digestive issues may arise due to stress or diet; health direction should focus on regular meals, eating slowly, and smaller portions. 'Collect the reins' also implies avoiding intense exercise; switch to gentle activities like walking or tai chi. 'Thousand paths' indicates many ways to regulate; you must discern for yourself, not follow trends. Be mindful of: frequent fatigue that sleep does not relieve, abnormal appetite, alternating irritability and depression—these are signs of excessive tension.
study: 'No going up' suggests that you may face a high point in your studies that is hard to break through in the short term; pushing may lead to frustration. Instead, 'turn back and realize'—review fundamentals, strengthen weak areas. 'Collect the reins well' metaphorically speaks to managing your study pace; avoid all-night cramming and fatigue warfare; create a balanced schedule. Da Zhuang transforming into Tai: initially you may have strong momentum but efficiency declines; changing your method and adjusting your mindset can lead to a state of fluency. 'Thousand paths' reminds you of multiple learning approaches—try mind maps, discussion recaps, error review, etc. Find the path that suits you. Exam luck is moderate; favor liberal arts and subjects requiring long-term accumulation. Prepare early, stay calm, and avoid last-minute changes to memorization materials.
general:
The essence of this sign is 'know when to stop' and 'choose another way.' The entire sign uses the horse's journey to illustrate that life always has many pathways; you need not cling to one. Three key actions: First, whenever you encounter difficulty or mental exhaustion, stop immediately, look back at your original intention and current situation, and decisively adjust your direction. Second, curb unnecessary external competitiveness, turn your energy inward, and do the work at hand—that is the best breakthrough. Third, actively broaden your horizons and explore overlooked parallel possibilities; do not walk a single path to the end. In mindset, remember that 'turning back and realizing' is not failure but wisdom. The thousand roads are not outside; they lie in your own calm choices. Uphold the softness of Kun Palace, adapt to circumstances, and you will naturally attain peace.

Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment.

sign_text:
Look in four directions, no gate or road;
Yet the Peach Blossom Spring path is open.
Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit,
Ten thousand years, a life like the pine.
interpretation1: The sign text 'Look in four directions, no gate or road; yet the Peach Blossom Spring path is open' is like being lost in a maze with walls on all sides. This 'no gate or road' is not a true dead end, but a metaphor for conventional paths being blocked and external conditions unfavorable. 'Peach Blossom Spring' alludes to the classic story of a hidden utopia, suggesting that the way out is not in noisy places but within, seeking tranquility. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' indicates that the way forward is not obtained for nothing; it requires physical and mental refinement, like the nine cycles of the alchemy furnace. 'Ten thousand years, a life like the pine' concludes with the image of pine trees enduring hardships, symbolizing that deep roots bring lasting blessings. The hexagram is from Li Palace: Meng (Youthful Folly) transforming into Wei Ji (Before Completion). Meng is mountain over water, a symbol of childish confusion awaiting enlightenment; Wei Ji is fire over water, with lines out of place, signifying that things are not yet complete but life is already stirring. Li is fire and south, with the fire-earth generating cycle, implying that wisdom light can penetrate the fog. Overall meaning: a trapped situation is not a death sentence; the Peach Blossom Spring is not in the sky, but in turning your thoughts and cultivating stillness. The key to breaking darkness is the word 'cultivation'; when sincerity is fulfilled, a pine-long life may be expected.
career: 'No gate or road' depicts a career impasse—the normal path for promotion is blocked, network support is thin, or you are stuck in a minor role or repeated failures. 'Yet the Peach Blossom Spring path is open' reveals that the turn lies in unconventional routes: consider shifting to niche fields, starting your own business, freelancing, or engaging in 'quiet' industries like culture, education, or public welfare. Meng transforming into Wei Ji: the hexagram shows mountain blocking water, fire over water yet hard to use—affairs may be repetitive, requiring patience to polish skills. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' is the foundation of your career: this sign favors advanced training, certification, and developing unique expertise. Avoid competing for positions or seeking quick fame. When the time comes, things will naturally fall into place. Recommended: Take on non-core projects that allow deep cultivation; develop cross-disciplinary skills. Avoid: Complaining about the environment, forcing opportunities that don't belong to you.
wealth: The sign 'Peach Blossom Spring path' cleverly metaphors wealth: not the noisy profits of the marketplace, but hidden growth in quiet places. This may include windfall gains such as antiques, artworks, forest and mountain products, or long-term equity investments that do not need immediate liquidation. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' warns against relying on luck; Meng hexagram means investment requires deep study and consideration; Wei Ji hexagram shows fire and water conflicting, so be cautious of financial plan errors—scrutinize accounts carefully and avoid credit expansion. 'Ten thousand years, a life like the pine' emphasizes long-lasting wealth: stable income is reliable, savings and accumulating funds; windfall gains may come occasionally, but avoid chasing highs and selling lows. Recommended: Allocate fixed-income assets, pay attention to agriculture and cultural creative industries. Avoid: Following speculative trends, mortgaging property for risky ventures.
love: 'No gate or road' maps emotional difficulties: singles feel like wandering a barren plain, meeting no kindred spirit; those in relationships feel estranged and out of sync. 'Yet the Peach Blossom Spring path is open' suggests that by avoiding noise and crowds, you may find the right connection—through refined gatherings, book clubs, ancient path travels, or in quiet spaces rekindling old bonds. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' emphasizes that love is not heaven-sent; it requires shared spiritual growth. Meng hexagram teaches tolerance for childishness; Wei Ji reminds that if harmony is not yet achieved, it must be gently adjusted. The pine longevity metaphor indicates lasting affection. Recommended for singles: Expand your circle of like-minded friends; avoid superficial speed dating. For couples: Learn a skill or travel together; avoid bringing up old grievances or forcing change.
health: Li Palace belongs to fire; Meng hexagram contains Kan water and Gen mountain. Attention is needed to disharmony between heart and kidney, and signs of water-dampness stagnation. 'No gate or road' may correspond to a feeling of blocked meridians, but 'Peach Blossom Spring path' implies that the door to health is not through heavy medication but quiet regulation, such as taking small paths to explore. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' directly guides internal cultivation practices like guiding exercises, meditation, or tai chi—persistent effort will yield longevity. The 'pine' symbolizes strength especially in autumn and winter; this sign indicates gradual improvement toward well-being. Old ailments should not be treated with harsh drugs; instead, eat bland, seasonal foods and keep a regular schedule. Be mindful of: mental exertion, staying up late damaging yin; signs like frequent mouth sores, soreness in lower back and knees—not diseases but body warnings. Early adoption of natural methods is advisable.
study: For studies, this sign resembles Meng hexagram's 'the foolish seek me.' 'No gate or road' may indicate feeling overwhelmed by subjects, lacking guidance, or blocked thinking. 'Yet the Peach Blossom Spring path is open' metaphorically suggests a unique path: besides mainstream subjects, those with special talents (arts, invention, niche fields) may stand out. 'Cultivate and refine to achieve the true fruit' emphasizes concentration and sustained effort; Wei Ji hexagram shows fire and water not yet united, so the worst is to scatter your energy—choose one field and go deep; eventually you will penetrate. Exam luck: favorable for self-designed practice or field-study learning; take the exam calmly, with the resilience of a pine tree unshaken by wind and frost. Recommended: Set long-term goals and break them into small steps; avoid comparing with others' speed; prefer studying in nature; avoid closing yourself in for rote memorization.
general:
The essence of this sign is 'change of thought' and 'focus.' When conventional paths are blocked, it is not that heaven has abandoned you but that you are invited to look inward. First, explore subtle paths—find the 'Peach Blossom Spring' in the ordinary, such as faint lights in relationships, industries, or interests. Second, cultivate quietly—whether skills, knowledge, or mind, steady polishing is your talisman. Third, maintain softness and long-term vision; the sign of pine longevity is not achieved overnight; do not contend for transient gains or losses. The hexagram Meng transforming into Wei Ji is like fire burning above water; though the potential is not yet realized, light has already permeated the waves. Thus, in mindset, avoid haste, resentment, and giving up. Hold a 'spirit of exploration,' believe that fragrant grass will grow green, and clear streams will guide themselves.

Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
No need to hurry, no need to fear,
A resting place will appear.
Where drums and bells resound,
The Three Jewels are near, at the gate.
interpretation1: The opening lines 'No need to hurry, no need to fear, a resting place will appear' offer spoken comfort, depicting a traveler suddenly finding a haven amid haste. Hurry and fear stem from a restless heart, but the 'resting place' suggests a stop in turmoil, implying that calmness brings opportunity. 'Where drums and bells resound' points to a venue of importance—temples, ceremonies, or solemn places—where opportunities manifest with ceremony, requiring serious attention. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' may refer to Buddhist treasures (Buddha, Dharma, Sangha) or precious guidance. They are close but not yet entered, reminding the seeker not to slacken just because something is within reach; discernment is needed to truly step inside. The hexagram belongs to Li Palace, from Lu to Li. Lu (Traveling, represented by Fire over Mountain) symbolizes wandering and rootlessness; transforming into Li (Double Fire, symbolizing clarity and attachment) brings brightness but also dependence. The sign is moderately unfavorable, with hidden harm within temporary peace. Overall meaning: Do not panic in difficulty; wait for clarity. Recognize precious guidance in solemn places, but do not lose independence through reliance, or the resting place may become a cage.
career: The line 'No need to hurry, no need to fear, a resting place will appear' suggests that rushing in career is futile; stability is better than change. The Lu hexagram indicates toil, and the transformation to Li suggests opportunities for dependence, but Li fire is illusory, implying that job changes or relying on others may be deceptive. 'Where drums and bells resound' signals important occasions—internal meetings, industry forums, or public selections—where opportunity hides in solemn ceremonies; prepare early and demonstrate strength. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' refers to benefactors, core projects, or a promotion that is close but not yet attained; take initiative without forcing. Li Palace belongs to Fire, favoring culture, media, energy, and internet industries; civil positions are more suitable than military. Moderately unfavorable sign advises: avoid blind job-hopping, aggressive competition, or joining unverified factions; instead, deepen your current position, showcase professionalism in public, and wait for the 'drums and bells' to sound naturally.
wealth: 'No need to hurry, no need to fear' directly advises against seeking wealth with urgency; 'a resting place will appear' indicates that regular income is stable, while speculative gains are illusory. 'Where drums and bells resound' suggests wealth opportunities may arise in lively venues—festivals, banquets, or cultural gatherings—but such gains are fleeting. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' implies precious wealth is close, but requires discernment; otherwise, the gate becomes a barrier. The hexagram Li is empty brightness; from Lu to Li is even more illusory. Wealth appears promising but is often empty. It is advisable to focus on stable earnings like salary or savings; consider long-term investments related to Li fire (e.g., cultural content, lighting technology), but avoid short-term speculation and high-interest schemes. Adopt a conservative attitude, choosing secure places without envying others' fanfare.
love: The sign's advice against hurry applies to love. 'A resting place will appear' hints at an eventual destination, but the transformation from Lu to Li suggests a relationship that is neither here nor deep. 'Where drums and bells resound' indicates that love may arise in lively settings—parties, weddings, or cultural events—and may become public quickly, yet requires calm reflection. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' means a precious person is close or a marriage stage is near, but feelings are not fully communicated; do not take it for granted. Li hexagram represents a middle-aged woman, suggesting the woman may take the lead, or both partners are strong-willed and prone to criticism. Moderately unfavorable sign: singles should not rush due to pressure; consider meeting through cultural or charitable events; beware of superficial enthusiasm. Couples should avoid losing themselves in dependence; the relationship needs substantive commitment—avoid old grievances or empty romance.
health: 'No need to hurry, no need to fear' is the first principle of health. The transformation from Lu to Li, fire upon fire, easily leads to heart fire rising, restlessness, and insomnia. 'A resting place' advises calming the mind and turning inward. 'Where drums and bells resound' corresponds to the ears and heart vessels—drum sounds like heartbeat, bell sounds like tinnitus, warning against palpitations, tinnitus, and hypertension from ascending deficiency fire. Li hexagram governs heart and small intestine, and in the senses, the eyes. Health maintenance should focus on clearing heart fire: keep a regular routine like the rhythm of drums—early to bed and early to rise; diet avoid spicy and greasy, eat more bitter-cooling foods such as bitter gourd and lotus seed heart; exercise should be gentle like stopping—tai chi and meditation are beneficial. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' metaphorically urges valuing the body as if about to enter a door; seek wise guidance for health practices but avoid superstition. Be alert to persistent insomnia, red eyes, dizziness—adjust and rest.
study: 'No need to hurry, no need to fear' points to the greatest taboo in study: haste and greed for more. 'A resting place' means building a solid foundation, not wandering. 'Where drums and bells resound' symbolizes important exams, defenses, or competitions—the sound of drums and bells is a wake-up call to take things seriously. 'The Three Jewels are near, at the gate' indicates that the path to knowledge is already visible, or that good teachers and friends are guiding you, like the Three Jewels showing the way, but entry still requires your own effort. Li hexagram represents civilization; from Lu to Li suggests moving from mixed to specialized knowledge. Study should focus on systematic research, favoring arts, media, and other Li fire fields. Exam luck may fluctuate; you may be easily disturbed at the test site; be sure to check the location in advance and adjust your schedule. Study in groups and seek advice from teachers; avoid working alone or last-minute cramming.
general: This sign emphasizes mindset above all. 'No need to hurry, no need to fear' is a guiding motto. Finding a resting place while traveling is temporary, not permanent. The transformation from the turmoil of Lu to the empty brightness of Li warns that what you seek is near the gate, yet you may lose autonomy through dependence. Three reminders: first, in action, do not rush; when facing a choice, pause for a few days to let the noise settle (after the drums and bells, there is silence) to see the truth; second, value opportunities from ceremonies and public occasions, but enter with real knowledge and ability, not surface efforts; third, treasures are near—discern whether they are true support or a cage. Guard your heart like jade, so you do not gain only to lose. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Busy all your life,
Always in vanity.
When impermanence comes, all things cease,
Turn back early, in good time.
interpretation1: The line 'Busy all life, but all is vanity' directly points to a lifetime of labor chasing shadows—all that is pursued is like flowers in a mirror. 'Vanity' implies fame and fortune are floating clouds without foundation. Then 'When impermanence comes, everything ends'—like a sudden storm, in an instant wealth, honor, love, and hate return to dust. 'Turn around quickly in time' is a sharp wake-up call to let go of attachments, turning from external pursuit to inner reflection. The hexagram from Li Palace: Wei Ji (Before Completion) transforms into Jin (Progress). Wei Ji shows fire and water not interacting, efforts futile; Jin indicates the sun rising above the earth, light gradually ascending. This transformation signals a shift from fruitless busyness to sober progress. Turning around is not retreat but shedding illusion to keep the real. The whole sign is very unfavorable, meaning that if one indulges in illusion, all ends in emptiness. Only by swiftly retreating and returning to one's origin can one avoid being devoured by impermanence.
career: 'Busy all life, but all is vanity' indicates labor without reward, busy in pointless competition or frequent changes. Li Palace Wei Ji: fire and water not interacting, symbolizing team discord and plans that fail to complete, leading to repeated starts without endings. The transformation to Jin suggests that the turn comes from letting go of grandiose ambitions. It is advisable to retract your focus, return to core skills or your original field, and cultivate deeply. Avoid changing jobs, starting businesses, or undertaking insubstantial projects. The timing is unfavorable in spring; stabilize in autumn and winter. Specific actions: review past failures to strengthen foundations; avoid chasing trends or engaging in pointless conflicts. If in management, avoid frequent changes; seek stability. Good fortune arises from turning back; abandon vanity to see the truth.
wealth: This sign is very unfavorable for wealth. 'Vanity' clearly indicates wealth like bubbles; chasing speculation or quick money will end in loss. Wei Ji hexagram: fire and water clash, financial sources blocked; Jin hexagram: fire and earth generate, turning to practical income brings hope.
