'A single rider breaks through many passes' symbolizes the need to overcome mental barriers or real difficulties, such as misunderstandings or distance—the lone rider suggests courageous confession. Good news is near; singles may receive an unexpected confession or contact from an old acquaintance; those in relationships may see affection deepen after trials. Kan (danger) transforming into Dui (joy) indicates joy after hardship. What to do: Reveal your true feelings honestly, face problems together. What to avoid: Repeated testing, cold war, suspicion; don't miss the window due to pride.
health: 'When called upon, act; when set aside, conceal' corresponds to the Kan image—Kan governs kidneys and ears, reminding care of kidney qi and hearing, and maintaining a rhythmic lifestyle, avoiding overindulgence and overwork. 'A single rider breaks through many passes' symbolizes breaking through illness; recovery is in sight, and good news may come from a check-up. The transformed hexagram Dui (Joy) governs the mouth and lungs; guard against autumn dryness harming the lungs. What to do: Tap teeth and swallow saliva in the morning to strengthen kidneys; eat black beans and yam; keep a regular sleep schedule. What to avoid: Spicy and fatty foods, excessive late nights. Watch for: tinnitus, lower back soreness, dry cough; timely rest can prevent chronic issues.
study: 'When called upon, act; when set aside, conceal' points to applying knowledge; currently, build a solid foundation rather than skimming the surface. 'A single rider breaks through many passes' indicates a key breakthrough; concentrate energy on conquering a weak subject single-handedly, such as mathematics or a foreign language—the lone rider symbolizes focused effort. Good news is near; significant improvement is likely before a major exam. The Jue hexagram emphasizes self-discipline; Dui favors oral exams and interviews. What to do: Set a rigid phased plan, complete tasks daily, actively seek guidance from teachers. What to avoid: Spreading efforts too thin, or stage fright. On exam day, arrive early; facing northeast may be favorable.
general: The overall message is about timing: first guard, then attack; first conceal, then act. You have reached the final pass of difficulty; good news is just a step away. The mystery of this sign lies in 'measure' and 'decision.' Actionable advice: 1. Observe quietly, wait for a clear signal (such as news from a benefactor or an emerging opportunity) before moving; 2. When you act, be independent and decisive, reducing reliance on others; 3. Pay attention to distant news or chance encounters, as they often carry turning points. Mentally, hold the composure of 'act when called, conceal when set aside,' and nurture the sharpness of 'a single rider through passes.' The smooth path is not far off.

sign_text:
Accumulate virtue and exert effort; descendants will follow.
Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits and to western neighbors.
When the two words 'merit and fame' are fully achieved,
Look back: mountains and rivers, all things renewed.
interpretation1: The four lines of this sign are interlinked: virtue as foundation, sacrifice as practice, fame as fruit, and renewal as vision, revealing the mixed nature of this sign.
career: The line 'Accumulate virtue and bestow effort' likens career to building a platform: layers of accumulation reach height. It clearly indicates that promotion comes not from clever tricks but from substantial virtue and solid work. Kan Palace's flowing water wisdom favors deep cultivation in professional fields like water conservancy, transportation, or consulting. 'Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits' uses the sincerity of sacrifice to warn: respect superiors, support juniors, share achievements timely, avoid monopolizing credit. Maintain harmonious rhythm with 'western neighbors' (partners, parallel departments) to gain their help at critical moments. 'When the two words 'merit and fame' are fully achieved' points to autumn/winter: project completions or performance assessments are likely to yield results. Prepare performance records early and report in detail. However, Ji Ji changing to Ming Yi means after success there may be brief confusion or personnel changes; avoid rushing for new achievements. Specific actions: Volunteer for difficult tasks requiring patient coordination; avoid being too sharp or arguing with superiors; for interviews or job changes, first solidify current position and accumulate reputation.
wealth: The sign 'Accumulate virtue and bestow effort' judges wealth as coming from proper means: salary and rewards grow with virtue and ability; do not take unjust wealth. 'Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits' implies wealth needs circulation and sharing. The farming image favors industries like land, grain, traditional manufacturing. Investments should be stable; avoid high-leverage speculation. 'When the two words 'merit and fame' are fully achieved' hints that wealth will follow career success, likely a few months after achievement. Ji Ji (Already Fulfilled) shows a temporary abundance but hidden consumption; Ming Yi warns not to show off wealth, guard against envious eyes. Financial management: live within means, set up a special reserve like sacrificial offerings, avoid lavish spending or blind partnerships. If possible, donate moderately to 'neighbors'—good deeds benefit long-term wealth sources.
love: 'Accumulate virtue and bestow effort; descendants will follow' resonates most with romance. Good relationships require good karma as soil and constancy as dew. Singles: do not complain about timing; first cultivate heart and do good deeds to attract a suitable partner. Those in relationships: love may have reached the stage of marriage and children. 'Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits and to western neighbors' reveals relationship maintenance: life rituals like anniversaries and visiting family are important; treat partner's friends and relatives well, especially 'western neighbors' representing opposite-sex close friends—maintain appropriate distance to avoid suspicion. 'When the two words 'merit and fame' are fully achieved' indicates the public announcement of love or wedding, synchronized with career success. Wait until both foundations are somewhat solid before grand ceremonies. Ji Ji changing to Ming Yi: after marriage, trivial matters may overshadow romance; avoid dredging up the past or competing for supremacy. Schedule regular couple time and exchange small gifts to keep warmth.
health: The sign 'Accumulate virtue and bestow effort' nourishes the heart; kind thoughts keep the five organs harmonious. Kan Palace governs kidneys; the change Ming Yi (fire entering earth) indicates insufficient kidney water and hyperactive heart fire—guard against insomnia, tinnitus, blood pressure fluctuations. 'Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits' directly addresses diet: grains and ox (warming nourishment) suggest meals with whole grains, less fatty sweets; the sincerity of sacrifice implies regular eating times, avoid gobbling. Ox needs labor; moderate movement like a steady ox: morning walks, tai chi are good. 'When fame is fully achieved' period may bring illness from overwork; Ming Yi hides underlying issues—suggest regular checkups. Specific: sleep before 11 p.m. to nourish kidneys; avoid late nights and worry; when feeling down, burn incense and sit quietly, emulating the reverence of sacrifice.
study: 'Accumulate virtue and bestow effort' warns that academics have no shortcuts; daily diligence like accumulating virtue, each point mastered like bestowing effort. Ji Ji (Already Fulfilled) indicates balance of arts and sciences, but Ming Yi (Darkening) means liberal arts thinking may fall into dead ends. 'Sacrifice grain and ox to the spirits' implies attitude toward study: respect teachers like gods, be amiable with classmates like neighbors, solve doubts through discussion. Ox's perseverance favors long-term memory; morning recitation and evening review are beneficial. 'When the two words 'merit and fame' are fully achieved' suggests exam luck becomes apparent after mid-autumn; long-term effort can yield high scores, but last-minute cramming is discouraged. Visit the exam site in advance, prepare stationery like sacrificial offerings. Set a plan and follow through step by step; avoid comparing progress with others or pulling all-nighters.
general: The essence of this sign is the cycle: accumulate virtue – bestow effort – gratitude – achievement – renewal. Fame is reachable, but not overnight. It requires goodness as the root, respect toward others, and diligence to make up for shortcomings. Key actions: 1. Check if your virtue has any deficits; correct them. 2. Proactively thank those who have helped you; maintain network. 3. When achievement comes, immediately gather brilliance and plan next steps to guard against the darkness of Ming Yi. Mentally, 'Look back: mountains and rivers, all things renewed' reminds: no matter how twisted the path, after the event, looking back, everything becomes a new landscape. Do not get stuck on immediate gains or losses; broaden your mind.

sign_text:
Safe and smooth, the road is level and wide.
A single wild goose flies amid the clouds.
Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm.
By the waterside, a woman weeps repeatedly.
interpretation1: The first line 'Safe and smooth, the road is level and wide' depicts a stable and peaceful path. The lone wild goose flying high in the clouds suggests that even in favorable circumstances, one may journey alone. 'Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm' alludes to a beautiful scene suddenly hit by a violent storm—matters of romance prone to unexpected waves. 'By the waterside, a woman weeps repeatedly' paints a picture of a woman weeping at the water's edge, pointing to emotional distress and unspeakable worries. The image sequence shifts from calm to solitary flight, then to storm and weeping—seemingly contradictory, yet reflecting the nature of life: fortune hidden within change. The hexagram originates from Zhen Palace; the primary hexagram, Jing (Well), symbolizes a well that never runs dry, suggesting self-cultivation and waiting. The changed hexagram Kan (Water) governs danger and emotion, indicating flowing feelings with a risk of sinking. Jing transforming into Kan is like well water surging into a river—meeting danger but eventually flowing freely. As a very favorable sign, overall peace and smoothness prevail, but emotions and relationships may bring loneliness and sudden changes. Hold steady and wait for the storm to pass.
career: The line 'Safe and smooth, the road is level and wide' indicates a stable career foundation with no major issues. However, 'A single wild goose flies amid the clouds' suggests working alone, lacking support—a sense of 'only one hand clapping.' The Jing hexagram advocates biding one's time; the change to Kan warns of sudden personnel changes or task shifts—especially 'sudden rainstorm' type disruptions that throw off rhythm. This sign advises consolidating and deepening current work, not rashly changing jobs or starting over. Timing: the wild goose flying in clouds indicates autumn/winter opportunities for overseas assignments, exchanges, or independent projects. Volunteer for tasks requiring solo responsibility, showcasing ability like a lone goose. Avoid: Over-reliance on teamwork, trusting verbal promises; keep written records, double-check important decisions. 'Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm' also hints that fame may be unexpectedly delayed, so prepare contingency plans.
wealth: 'Safe and smooth, the road is level and wide' suggests steady income; 'a single wild goose flies amid the clouds' hints at windfall gains that require active pursuit—often distant or mobile sources like remote business or online marketing. 'Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm' warns of sudden financial risks; avoid following trends. Peach blossoms blooming then storm—seemingly prosperous short-term investments may be hit by bad news. The hexagram Jing (Well) represents a source of wealth, Kan (Water) indicates gathering and scattering unpredictably. Wealth trend: first stable, then fluctuating; early savings are secure, later watch for unexpected expenses like the weeping by the waterside. Conservative financial strategy advised: preservation over expansion; avoid debt. Specific fields: water transport, logistics, water conservancy, or stable fixed-income products. Avoid flashy 'peach blossom' industries like beauty or decoration.
love: This sign has strong emotional imagery. 'Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm' directly points to sudden turbulence in romantic affairs, such as misunderstandings, family opposition, or brief separation; 'By the waterside, a woman weeps repeatedly' adds sorrow, explicitly portraying a woman weeping—but actually indicates both parties may hurt each other through emotional arguments. For singles, peach blossoms appear but may be scattered by the 'storm'—new connections may cool quickly, so don't rush. The wild goose in the clouds suggests first communicate slowly through letters/messages; avoid jumping in with full heart. For those in relationships, the weeping by the water implies one side is holding back grievances. Avoid dredging up the past or arguing in front of friends and family. For those in crisis, this sign is not a sign of breakup; the rain will clear. A short trip or handling a sudden matter together can rebuild trust. Zhen Palace moves, Kan represents danger; relationship strategy: respond to danger with the stability of a level road—use fewer harsh words, more acts of care.
health: The 'sudden rainstorm' in the sign indicates external invasion, so guard against seasonal colds or acute gastrointestinal issues. Jing (Well) transforming into Kan (Water): Jing belongs to wood (dredging), Kan belongs to water, governs kidneys, urinary system, and ears. Health focus: nourish kidneys and promote water metabolism. The wild goose in the clouds—clouds are water vapor—implies dampness not dispelled, leading to dizziness or joint heaviness. 'Woman weeping' indicates grief harming lungs and worry harming spleen, so watch for loss of appetite or dream-disturbed sleep due to emotional fluctuations. Dietary direction: warm and light foods, more winter melon, coix seed to dispel dampness; avoid raw, cold, greasy. Regular schedule: avoid 'sudden storm' irregular late nights; sleep before 11 p.m. to nourish kidney qi. Exercise: level walking, as in 'smooth road'; avoid heavy sweating in wind. Warning signs: sudden tinnitus, sore lower back, abnormal urination—adjust promptly.
study: 'Safe and smooth, the road is level and wide' suggests a decent academic foundation with a stable routine, but 'a single wild goose flies amid the clouds' warns of falling out of sync with peers, feeling isolated and unmotivated. Migration of geese favors self-study or studying abroad; proactively place yourself in new environments to stimulate drive. 'Peach blossoms meet a sudden rainstorm' implies exam room surprises—sudden question changes, misreading—so practice adaptability. Jing (Well) to Kan (Water): Jing symbolizes accumulation, Kan symbolizes traps. Learning requires daily in-depth drilling like digging a well; avoid last-minute cramming. Subject advantage: Jing favors liberal arts; Kan adds some risk in math and logic—prioritize solidifying basic concepts. Specific methods: make clear plans like a level road; to guard against 'sudden rain,' set aside time each day to review mistakes. Avoid relying on exam predictions or pulling all-nighters.
general: This sign is overall auspicious with a note of caution: first showing smooth road and high flight, then peach blossom storm and woman weeping—it reminds that in good times, guard against emotional undercurrents and sudden changes. Jing transforming into Kan indicates a shift from quiet dwelling to flowing adjustment. Mentally, hold the steadiness of a well and the flexibility of water. Three key actions: 1. Like the wild goose, remain independent and alert; prepare contingency plans during stable periods. 2. Like avoiding the storm, when facing sudden personnel or financial fluctuations, observe first then respond; don't make decisions at emotional peaks. 3. To resolve the 'weeping by the water,' communicate gently with those around you; don't bottle up worries. Remember: the sign's closing is not despair—after rain comes clear sky, after tears comes laughter. Hold this thought, and you will pass through safely.

sign_text:
Within the gate, weapons clash—
Kin and foe, both in discord.
When the scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon,
Only then will you laugh with joy.
interpretation1: The first line, 'Within the gate, weapons clash,' symbolizes sudden internal strife, like family discord or colleagues turning against each other—'weapons' indicate sharp conflict. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' further emphasizes blurred lines between close and distant, gratitude and grievance—like the Meng hexagram's obscurity blinding the heart, leading to mutual suspicion. The next two lines take a sharp turn: 'When the scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' is vivid imagery—scarlet robes are court attire, sun and moon represent bright times or a wise ruler, indicating a benefactor bringing light to dispel clouds. 'Only then will you laugh with joy' depicts clouds parting, grudges melting, and happiness restored. The hexagram belongs to Li Palace (fire, civilization), Meng (ignorance) transforming into Sun (decrease). This indicates initial confusion causing loss, but eventually, wisdom arrives and loss turns to gain. As a moderately favorable to very favorable sign, it implies that although conflict arises from within, external force or timing will resolve it. Those who laugh last must first endure hardship; do not abandon hope due to temporary difficulty.
career: The line 'Within the gate, weapons clash' directly points to internal workplace conflicts: departmental infighting, colleagues vying for recognition. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' suggests difficulty distinguishing friend from foe; watch for hidden arrows. Meng (Ignorance) transforming into Sun (Decrease) indicates initial obscurity and loss—project setbacks or obstacles. But 'scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' signals a turning point: the scarlet robe symbolizes a higher-level benefactor, authoritative approval, or favorable policy; sun and moon represent a clear situation. Patiently wait for senior intervention or a key moment (year-end review, organizational restructuring). During this time, avoid taking sides or challenging superiors. Instead, keep a low profile, sharpen professional skills, and the benefactor's favor will naturally bring a thaw. Favorable roles: support or technical positions requiring endurance and cooperation. Avoid: grabbing credit or leading battles.
wealth: The sign 'Within the gate, weapons clash' warns that wealth may be damaged by interpersonal friction, such as partnership disputes or family inheritance fights. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' indicates mixed financial energies, hard to distinguish proper from windfall, and likely loss due to emotional obligations. Meng (Ignorance) transforming into Sun (Decrease): Meng suggests vulnerability to deception, Sun indicates loss; investments and partnerships may face traps, with early losses. 'Scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' is a breakthrough omen: the scarlet robe represents noble qi, sun and moon represent clarity—hinting that a benefactor or policy tailwind will turn losses into gains, bringing laughter. Wealth strategy: first defend then attack; stable income is safe; windfall may come after autumn/winter clarity. Favor earth or fire industries (real estate, energy). Avoid high-risk speculation. Focus on clearing accounts and stopping losses, not expanding new projects.
love: The sign 'Within the gate, weapons clash' mirrors family or couple quarrels and accumulated resentment like weapons. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' shows love and hate intertwined, hard to handle—like the Meng hexagram's obscurity. Sun (Decrease) hexagram indicates emotional wear; stubbornness will hurt both. 'Scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' suggests a mediator (elder) or an opportune moment (festival, shared event) to resolve the deadlock. The scarlet robe is festive, sun and moon are harmonious light; the stalemate will dissolve, leading to 'laugh with joy' and restored harmony. For singles, first resolve inner conflicts; wait for the right chance (e.g., meeting with a benefactor); don't force in confusion. Relationship advice: avoid digging up the past or cold wars; use an auspicious day to be honest and make the first friendly gesture.
health: The line 'Within the gate, weapons clash' symbolizes internal energy disorder, such as liver fire attacking lungs or heart-kidney imbalance. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' indicates yin-yang disharmony, mixed cold and heat. Meng (Ignorance) to Sun (Decrease): Meng represents stagnation; Sun indicates consumption of vitality—likely due to overthinking causing illness. 'Scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' signals a turn toward recovery: the scarlet robe represents blood and complexion; sun and moon represent balance. Focus on harmonizing yin-yang and regulating blood. Watch for: insomnia, dizziness, chest tightness. Calm the mind through practices like tai chi or baduanjin. Diet: avoid spicy and stimulating foods; face east in the morning and breathe deeply to nourish the liver. Key signs: red eyes, bitter taste, rib pain—don't neglect emotional regulation.
study: The line 'Within the gate, weapons clash' signifies conflicting thoughts or indecision in studies, like struggling between arts and sciences. 'Kin and foe, both in discord' indicates a scattered knowledge system, hard to prioritize—entering the obscurity of Meng hexagram. Sun (Decrease) hexagram suggests wasted effort, possible weak subjects or hitting a plateau. 'Scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon' is a breakthrough sign: the scarlet robe represents a wise teacher or authoritative text; sun and moon represent wisdom. Seek guidance from a mentor or seize an exam/defense opportunity to break through. Favor liberal arts and social sciences; build foundation with classics. Exam luck improves around summer/autumn. Avoid studying in isolation; participate in seminars. Review past mistakes before exams—then 'laugh with joy' for results.
general: This sign hides a turning point within conflict, first loss then gain. The key is 'scarlet-robed one approaches sun and moon': the scarlet robe is a benefactor or timing; sun and moon are clarity. It reminds not to be trapped by immediate conflict; hold to righteousness and wait for the clouds to part. Actionable advice: 1. Cease internal strife; reflect and restrain yourself—don't escalate conflict. 2. Build your strength so you are ready when the scarlet-robed one arrives. 3. Trust the way of heaven; the laugh of joy will surely follow endurance. Mentally, be like the fire of Li Palace—though temporarily covered in ash, it will eventually blaze. Do not lose your inner light.

sign_text:
A person captures a deer,
Matters draw to a round.
Yet outside the door,
A claim is pressed.
interpretation1: The sign uses the image of capturing a deer, a symbol of fortune and opportunity, suggesting initial success. However, 'outside the door a demand is made' warns of potential envy or claims from others. The hexagram 'Tai' (Peace) transforming into 'Xu' (Waiting) indicates that while things seem harmonious, one must remain cautious, guard against greed, and wait patiently to secure lasting benefit.
career: In career, 'capturing a deer' indicates achieving an important position or recognition. Yet 'outside demand' reminds you that success may invite rivalry or extra pressure. The hexagram suggests that while opportunity exists, it is advisable to share credit, work humbly, and avoid flaunting achievements. This period favors coordination, logistics, and earth-related industries (real estate, agriculture). Avoid monopolizing credit or rejecting others' requests harshly. The turning point may come around summer-autumn transition; handle follow-ups patiently.
wealth: Wealth is indicated by the deer, suggesting gains from hard work or rewards. However, 'outside demand' warns of loans, sharing profits, or unexpected expenses. The hexagram 'Tai' to 'Xu' suggests that financial luck tends to stagnate; better to focus on stable income rather than risky investments. Favor industries like land, agriculture, storage. Avoid showing off wealth, being a guarantor, or trusting partnerships easily. Set aside reserves for social obligations.
love: In love, capturing a deer signifies emotional fulfillment, and 'reunion' suggests harmonious relationships. Singles may find a suitable partner; couples enjoy family happiness. Yet 'outside demand' introduces external factors: opinions from elders, misunderstandings with friends, or feelings of uneven giving. The hexagram advises patience and listening. Avoid dwelling on past mistakes or cold wars. For singles, appreciate steady companions, not illusions.
health: The deer relates to liver, tendons, and vitality; 'outside demand' suggests external drains on energy. The hexagram indicates attention to digestion and kidneys. No major issues, but overwork or improper diet may cause bloating or backache. Eat warm, light meals (yellow millet, yam). Get enough sleep, avoid staying up late. Notice signals like stomach discomfort or heaviness.
study: In studies, capturing a deer represents passing exams or research breakthroughs. Yet 'outside demand' means higher expectations from teachers or peers. The hexagram advises consolidating basics and avoiding overambition. Favor in-depth study of classics, history, philosophy. Prepare thoroughly before exams; avoid last-minute cramming. Exam performance is stable but watch for careless errors.
general: This sign symbolizes that gain and loss coexist; with achievement comes potential claim. The key is humility and caution: share your gains, assess external demands without anger or fear, and make decisions slowly. Remember that 'outside demand' is a normal part of life; handling it with wisdom turns moderate luck into lasting peace. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Your going brings no gain,
Flowers bloom only to meet the autumn.
Harsh frost falls upon all things in waves,
If you do not retreat, no foothold will remain.
interpretation1: The sign warns that moving forward is unfavorable, as flowers have reached autumn—prosperity is fleeting. 'Harsh frost' symbolizes continuous adversity; 'retreat leaves no foothold' emphasizes that without timely withdrawal, even your current position may be lost. The hexagram 'Meng' (Youthful Ignorance) transforming into 'Gu' (Decay) suggests confusion and internal deterioration. The message is to stop, reflect, and retreat to preserve yourself.
career: In career, this sign indicates a difficult period. Avoid seeking changes or competing for projects. Past glory is fading; now focus on consolidating your current role. External environment may be harsh with industry changes or office politics. Best to lie low, improve skills quietly, and avoid confrontations. Favor behind-the-scenes work. Avoid challenging authority or expanding responsibilities.
wealth: Financially, the sign advises against pursuing wealth now. Any gains will be short-lived, and losses may follow. Unexpected expenses or hidden leaks may appear. Retreat and conserve: audit your assets, cut expenses, suspend risky investments. Avoid lending, guarantees, or new partnerships. Stick to stable income from regular job.
love: In love, the relationship may cool down or face external pressures. Passion fades like autumn flowers; disputes or misunderstandings may increase. The hexagram suggests communication is stuck. If single, starting a new romance now is unfavorable—focus on clarifying your own feelings. For couples, give each other space, avoid arguing over past issues, and jointly face practical problems.
health: Health: 'harsh frost' indicates recurring illnesses or relapse, especially respiratory issues. The hexagram points to potential insomnia, depression, or digestive troubles. Do not overstrain; rest more, sleep early. Eat warm, nourishing food. Gentle exercise like walking or qigong. Watch for minor ailments that persist; do not ignore them.
study: Studies: You may face a plateau or confusion. Do not push too hard; instead, review fundamentals and correct mistakes. External pressures like exam changes or competition may increase. Maintain steady pace, avoid switching strategies last minute. Favor liberal arts, but results may be mediocre. Focus on mental calm.
general: This sign emphasizes retreat as the key strategy. When facing adversity, stop forcing progress. Protect your current position, cut losses, and reflect on hidden problems. Use this period to clear out old issues and await a better time. Remember, retreat is not cowardice but preservation. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
A crescent moon as hook,
A clear breeze as line.
Raise the net on misty waves,
Splendid fish are easy to find.
interpretation1: The sign paints a picture of a fisherman using the crescent moon as a hook and the breeze as a line, calmly casting his net on misty waters to catch splendid fish easily. It suggests that while conditions are not perfect, you can turn existing resources to your advantage. The hexagram 'Sheng' (Ascending) transforming into 'Tai' (Peace) indicates rising momentum leading to harmony. Act with creativity and confidence.
career: In career, this sign encourages taking initiative despite limited resources. Use your network, reputation, and information as 'breeze' lines. Plan thoroughly before acting. The hexagram points to promotion opportunities and team harmony. Favor roles that require mobility, coordination, or innovation. Avoid fear of responsibility or passive waiting. Summer-autumn is favorable.
wealth: Wealth prospects are favorable. The crescent moon suggests small-scale investment can yield returns. Windfall gains are possible through side jobs or unexpected rewards. The hexagram advises maintaining agility but avoiding high-risk unknowns. Regular salary may increase. Favor industries like education, water conservancy, logistics. Avoid impulsive partnerships or excessive debt.
love: In love, the crescent moon symbolizes a fresh start or rekindled romance. Singles may meet an attractive person through social or artistic events. Couples will find harmony and can plan shared activities like travel. Keep communication light and flexible. Avoid possessiveness or dwelling on the past.
health: Health outlook is good, with balanced energy. Suitable exercises include tai chi or qigong. Maintain regular sleep and eat green vegetables to support liver health. Avoid overindulgence. Keep a calm mindset.
study: Studies: Focus on core concepts and connect them into a framework like a net. Review with mind maps. Exam fortune is favorable; you will perform confidently. This period suits arts and creative subjects. Study in quiet times like early morning. Discuss with peers to gain insights.
general: This sign radiates positivity but stresses action: use the crescent hook and breeze line—start with what you have. Seize the 'almost perfect' moment; utilize intangible resources like reputation and relationships. Stay calm amidst complexity. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
First lock the doors,
Then stay on guard.
If minor details are not heeded,
Complacency will lead to great disaster.
interpretation1: The sign emphasizes precaution: 'lock the doors' and 'stay on guard' symbolize closing off external threats and constant vigilance. Neglecting small details can accumulate into disaster. The hexagram 'Mingyi' (Darkening of the Light) suggests hidden troubles; its transformation into 'Jiji' (Already Fulfilled) indicates that while things seem complete, hidden dangers remain. The key is to be meticulous and avoid complacency.
career: In career, secure your current position and avoid overstepping. Be alert to office politics and potential sabotage. Double-check all documents and communications. Avoid verbal agreements; clarify responsibilities in projects. Do not discuss work in casual settings. Improvements may come in late autumn; for now, focus on internal strength.
wealth: Wealth: The sign warns of financial losses through hidden traps. Consolidate your assets, avoid investments and risky partnerships. Pay attention to account details, contracts, and credit. Favor earth-related industries (real estate, agriculture) with conservative management. Refuse high-interest temptations, especially from acquaintances. Monthly reconciliation is advised.
love: In love, this sign suggests emotional distance and potential misunderstandings. Minor disagreements can escalate if left unaddressed. Singles should be cautious about new relationships—verify intentions. Couples need open communication; establish ground rules for arguments. Avoid cold wars and suspicion. Married couples: beware of external temptations and financial disputes.
health: Health: The hexagram indicates imbalance between heart and kidneys. Nourish with black beans, mulberries, lotus seeds. Maintain regular sleep, especially midday rest. Gentle exercise only. Watch for overall signs of weakness.
study: Studies: Focus on undistracted study. Review basic formulas and consolidate knowledge. Avoid overconfidence. Build a mistake notebook and simulate exam conditions. Favor liberal arts but beware of subjective bias. Exam luck is moderate; spring and winter are slightly better. Stay calm and double-check each step.
general: This sign revolves around caution in small matters. Conduct daily self-checks on details like deadlines, amounts, and communication. Guard your words in interpersonal relations. When in doubt, break from routine habits—complacency is the root of major troubles. Maintain an inner calm and outward humility. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Swallows chirp and orioles sing,
Fresh flowers fill the courtyard.
Leaning on the rail, your spring sleep ends;
Wordless, a worried brow gathers.
interpretation1: The sign paints a picture of spring splendor and vitality: swallows and orioles as harbingers of good news, flowers symbolizing fulfillment and prosperity. The Dui Palace governs joy and communication; the Qian hexagram suggests humility. Yet the latter half shifts: leaning on the rail after a nap, a silent worried brow—hinting that humility without action may lead to difficulty (Jian hexagram: water on a mountain, obstacles). Overall, favorable times require vigilance; ease may hide future worry. The sign is mixed but tends favorable if one remains alert.
career: Swallows and orioles suggest harmonious workplace relations and timely information; flowers blooming indicate projects bearing fruit. However, "leaning on the rail, spring sleep" warns against complacency. The Qian hexagram's humility is good, but if excessive, it may lead to stagnation. Dui Palace favors communication; Jian hexagram advises against reckless advancement. During success, review plans actively. Avoid: indulging in socializing, delaying decisions, shirking responsibility. Favorable trend; acting in harmony with circumstances can avert the "worried brow."
wealth: Blooming flowers suggest diverse income or small investment gains; swallows and orioles also hint at cooperative earnings or profit from communication. But "spring sleep" warns against relaxing financial diligence. Jian hexagram's water (danger) suggests unexpected expenses may arise from neglect. Regular income may see steady rise; speculative gains should be taken quickly. Avoid greedy high-interest borrowing or following trends. Dui Palace indicates some loss; spend moderately. Preserve cash flow like holding back worry; ensure monthly peace.
love: Swallows and orioles, flowers blooming are good omens for romance. Singles may meet someone congenial through social activity. Yet "leaning on the rail, spring sleep, wordless worried brow" depicts a languid sentiment: perhaps nostalgia or unresolved ambiguity. The Qian hexagram advises reserve, but transforming into Jian suggests communication issues may cause misunderstandings. For those in relationships, avoid brooding over trifles. Take a spring outing to break the ice. Moderate to favorable love luck; avoid dwelling on past grievances, actively express feelings to prevent worry.
health: The spring sleep imagery, Dui Palace (lungs, throat), and Jian hexagram (water obstruction) warn against excessive spring lethargy or minor respiratory issues. Flowers may trigger allergies. Adjust sleep schedule, don't over-nap causing qi stagnation. Eat light, moistening foods for lungs. The worried brow suggests emotional strain affecting liver and spleen. Listen to natural sounds like birdsong for comfort. No major illness, but do not ignore minor signs. The sign advises early to bed and early to rise.
study: Swallows and orioles represent active academic exchange; good to heed teachers and peers for breakthroughs. Flowers blooming symbolize accumulated knowledge showing results; exam prep may bear fruit. However, "spring sleep" hints at distraction or laziness—the Qian hexagram's humility turned to inertia, causing Jian-like difficulties: familiar topics become elusive. Exams favor liberal arts and oral tests; avoid overconfidence. Transform worry into focus; replace nap time with morning study. Then fortune improves.
general: This moderately favorable sign pairs spring beauty with sorrow, reminding: in times of plenty, remain humble; in ease, stay vigilant. Act on the momentum of swallows and flowers to advance interpersonal affairs, but guard against 'spring sleep' inertia. Communicate actively to melt ice (Dui Palace) and resolve hidden difficulties (Jian hexagram). Daily: make clear lists to avoid procrastination; nurture key relationships, avoid solitary pride; when worried, walk outdoors to absorb the vitality of birds. Do not be elated by smooth sailing nor fear temporary setbacks; all depends on self-control. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Toil your heart, toil your heart,
For such toil will surely bear fruit.
A clear breeze lends you strength,
And laughter lies ahead on your journey.
interpretation1: The repeated 'toil your heart' emphasizes inevitable mental exertion and hard work; 'toil will bear fruit' promises eventual reward but only through sustained effort. 'Clear breeze lends strength' suggests opportunity arrives like a gentle wind—not forceable, requiring patience. 'Laughter ahead' envisions a positive outcome, yet 'lends strength' implies success is not entirely within one's control. The hexagram changes from Qian (humility) to Kun (earth, receptivity). This transformation teaches that only by humbly enduring labor can one harness Kun's receptivity and resilience to turn difficulties around. Overall: though 'moderately unfavorable,' it is not hopeless; it advises restraint, diligent work, and waiting for the opportune breeze.
career: 'Toil your heart' directly points to current heavy workload and mental strain. 'Toil will bear fruit' indicates that efforts will eventually pay off, but timing is not yet ripe; patience is needed. 'Clear breeze' is a turning point—opportunities or supporters appear unexpectedly; forcing may cause misses. Qian transforming to Kun warns against competing for credit; use softness to overcome rigidity. Short-term career may have fluctuations; focus on deepening skills, solidifying foundation, avoid impulsive job changes or conflict. Autumn/winter may bring the 'breeze' (e.g., internal transfer, project support). Suitable for collaborative roles. Do: take on behind-the-scenes tasks, be modest. Avoid: rushing to claim credit, arguing with superiors, overacting in groups.
wealth: 'Toil will bear fruit' indicates wealth comes through hard work, not speculation. 'Clear breeze' suggests financial improvement relies on intangible forces like connections or policy trends. Qian to Kun: Dui Palace (Metal) transforms to Kun (Earth); Earth generates Metal, so long-term prospects are grounded, but current focus is on stability. Regular income stable; avoid speculative gains. Favorable for positions, technical consulting, service revenue. Avoid risk investments. Save conservatively; avoid following hype or partnerships without thorough planning. When the 'breeze' arrives (e.g., key info or reliable cooperation), small moves are possible, but maintain humility, share benefits. Avoid greed for quick profits or leveraging.
love: 'Toil your heart' often reflects restless longing or hidden tension in relationships. 'Clear breeze' advises not to force—give space for turnaround. Qian hexagram (mountain hidden in earth) suggests emotions should be deep and hidden; avoid rushing to claim possession. Singles: forcing now unhelpful; meet naturally through interests or friends; good news may come after autumn. Those in stable relationships: guard against repeated arguments; learn Kun's tolerance; yield for peace. Those on the brink: postpone accusations; use actions rather than words, wait for calm. Do: cultivate self, allow solitude. Avoid: dwelling on past, checking whereabouts, rushing to define relationship.
health: 'Toil the heart' directly indicates mental overwork, worry damaging the spleen; Dui Palace governs mouth, lungs; Qian hexagram has suppressing quality, easily causing qi stagnation, chest tightness, insomnia. Kun represents abdomen; change to Kun (Earth) warns of digestive issues. The clear breeze though refreshing may bring pathogenic wind; avoid catching chill. Health direction: calm the mind, nourish qi—walk, light qigong, meditation. Avoid strenuous dissipation. Diet: sweet and mild foods (millet, yam) to strengthen spleen; reduce worry, anger. Warning signs: headache, throat discomfort, erratic appetite—signs of overexertion; regulate rest schedule, don't push through.
study: 'Toil your heart, toil will bear fruit' describes diligent study; no shortcuts, need patient accumulation. 'Clear breeze' suggests using efficient methods (mind maps, teacher guidance) but not relying entirely. Qian to Kun: good for solidifying basics, low-key revision, not for rushed cramming. Suitable for segmenting difficult topics, focusing on error review and fundamentals. Exam luck: favorable for inspired subjects (essays, discussions), but only with sufficient preparation. Favor humanities and subjects requiring insight; math and logic need steady effort. Do: set regular schedule, study in groups. Avoid: all-nighters, sacrificing sleep, last-minute cramming.
general: The sign begins with 'toil' and ends with 'laughter,' revealing that bitterness leads to sweetness. This moderately unfavorable sign is not a permanent trap but a reminder to now gather heart, store strength, and wait for the uncontrollable 'breeze.' Key points: first, accept the necessity of toil without resentment; second, cultivate humility during lows—like Kun earth's receptivity, it will eventually support joy. Action: daily self-reflection, reduce useless socializing; actively undertake unnoticed tasks that build expertise; watch for subtle shifts in environment—seemingly accidental yet inevitable opportunities. Mentally, view 'toil' as refinement; believe 'fruit' will come; when the breeze arrives, you will be ready. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
Advancing yet hesitant,
The spring wind blows the willow fluff.
By the waterside, a traveler grows weary,
While at the pillow, deep worries linger.
interpretation1: The sign 'advancing yet hesitant' implies progress is mixed with hesitation, like willow fluff blown by spring wind—unstable. 'Spring wind blows willow fluff' points to season: spring wind brings life, but willow fluff is rootless, suggesting opportunity and illusion coexist. 'By the waterside a traveler tires' depicts fatigue on a journey, warning of mid-process exhaustion. 'At the pillow worries linger' indicates nighttime anxiety and overthinking. The Kun Palace (earth, receptivity), Fu hexagram (one yang returning, renewal), changing to Yi (nourishment). Overall: this moderately favorable sign hides a 'difficult yet restful' message: things progress but waver, the heart wants to move but is weary. Only quiet cultivation can turn worry to peace.
career: Avoid: indulging in excessive socializing, gossiping, or speculating about market movements.
wealth: Willow fluff imagery indicates illusory wealth; speculative matters may have twists, seeming opportunities but hard to grasp. 'Waterside traveler tired' points to hard-earned money with fatigue; returns may not match effort. Kun Palace supports stable regular income, especially earth-related industries (real estate, storage, agriculture). Fu hexagram's repetition warns of fluctuating finances; avoid large in-and-out. Yi hexagram nourishes wealth through savings, long-term low-risk investments. Do: set long-term accumulation plans, reduce non-essential spending. Avoid: following market hype, investing in unclear partnerships. 'Pillow worries' hints financial anxiety often disturbs sleep; ease the mind, wait until autumn for improvement.
love: Emotionally, 'spring wind willow fluff' suggests a restless, unstable state. 'Advancing yet hesitant' means relationship push-pull, or one hesitates to commit, or mismatched pace. Singles: easy to fall into ambiguous situations; lively but rootless romances. 'Waterside traveler tired' implies exhaustion from pursuit; pause to examine heart. Those with partners: 'pillow worries' directly points to hidden concerns affecting intimacy. Kun hexagram advises tolerance; listen more, argue less. Yi hexagram nourishes relationship with daily care. Suitable: shared walks, tea, calming activities; avoid: digging up past, pressuring for decisions.
health: Health sign centers on 'pillow worries'—anxiety harming the spleen, Fu hexagram's repetition suggests neurasthenia, insomnia, digestive issues. Kun rules abdomen, Yi relates to diet; focus on digestive care. 'Waterside traveler tired' links to kidneys (water); exhaustion hints at depleted energy, watch for lower back soreness. Spring wind willow fluff may trigger allergies; guard against seasonal colds. Regimen: soft, warm food eaten when calm; millet, yam to nourish earth. Rest by 10pm to nurture Fu's yang. Exercise: gentle walks, qigong; avoid intense workouts. Warning signs: recurrent mouth ulcers, difficulty sleeping, persistent worry—signals to cultivate the mind; try meditation.
study: 'Advancing yet hesitant' mirrors study where progress is slow despite effort. Willow fluff suggests distraction, easily disturbed. 'Waterside traveler tired' fits study fatigue, fluctuating efficiency. Kun Palace favors steady accumulation; Fu hexagram values review; this sign benefits revision, not aggressive new study. Specific: review weak points, revisit old lessons—often gain insights. Exam luck: watch for hesitation, especially multiple-choice revisions. Yi hexagram nourishes wisdom; avoid staying up late; ensure sleep to restore brain. Subjects: this sign leans toward humanities, literature, history; math logic requires extra focus. Before exam, calm the mind to avoid environmental distractions.
general: The essence of the sign is 'nurture.' Moderately favorable does not mean all good; it teaches to recognize the opportunity for nurturing amidst hesitation and fatigue. Willows are chaotic but spring wind is orderly; the waterside may be tiring but the shore beckons. Kun Palace, Fu to Yi: the earth's receptivity, one yang renews, final peace. Specific advice: first, suspend expansion—consolidate existing foundations; second, deal with worries: write down concerns, analyze them in daylight; third, get close to water and woods—sit by water, view greenery to calm the mind. Mentally, do not doubt yourself for 'hesitation'; it is normal before precipitation. Willow fluff returns to earth, the tired traveler returns home—everything comes in due time. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Jade and stone still have flaws.
How many small regrets can one bear?
In the end, no blame.
Laugh heartily.
interpretation1: The sign compares to fine jade with hidden flaws, implying human affairs are rarely perfect; even the worthy may be overlooked or commit small errors. 'How many small regrets can one bear?' advises not to over-criticize oneself for trivial mistakes. 'In the end no blame' asserts final safety, no disaster. 'Laugh heartily' closes with bright cheer, indicating a comforting outcome. The hexagram changes from Bo (peeling, erosion) to Gen (mountain, stillness). Bo's five yin erode one yang; transforming to Gen stops the erosion. This matches the sign: despite flaws and small regrets, knowing when to stop and stay correct ensures safety. This sign is auspicious. Core message: seek completeness in imperfection; accept flaws, don't let small losses ruin the big picture; wait calmly for turnaround.
career: Take the 'jade and stone still have flaws' imagery: minor mistakes at work, talent unrecognized, or project results slightly lacking. 'Small regrets' remind not to be trapped by trivial errors, nor shift blame. Bo to Gen indicates turning from loss to stop; career should stabilize and review, not rush expansion. Specific: those in closing or handover phases should check details carefully to avoid oversight. For those in competition or assessment, as jade with slight flaw, no harm—focus on core competence, not perfection. 'In the end no blame' promises harmless outcome despite waves. Do: make contingency plans, learn from 'small regrets.' Avoid: over-caution, or covering mistakes for credit.
wealth: Wealth like flawed jade suggests hidden defects: ambiguous contract terms, unclear accounts, minor overlooked investments. 'Small regrets' hints at remorse over small losses, but 'in the end no blame' shows no major loss. Bo to Gen: Bo is consumption, Gen is stop—on financial path, cut losses in time, don't cling to small gains. Steady approach, prudent accounting, check details. Avoid blind following. Favor regular income, guard against traps in speculation. Specific: check agreements line by line, especially partnerships/loans; avoid convenience over proper procedure. 'Laugh heartily' hints financial peace after minor regrets.
love: In love, 'jade and stone still have flaws' means both parties have unnoticed strengths and weaknesses, or minor misunderstandings. 'Small regrets' warns against quarreling over trifles, which only brings regret. 'In the end no blame' indicates no fundamental conflict; ending harmony. Bo to Gen: Bo suggests distancing, Gen stops it—cold wars or rifts will resolve before worsening. Singles may meet someone with minor flaws but good character; relax standards, focus on essence. Those in relationships: communicate actively, tolerate small faults—flaw does not diminish jade's beauty. 'Laugh heartily' points to reconciliation. Do: honest communication; avoid: seeking perfection, denying whole for small flaw.
health: 'Jade and stone still have flaws' corresponds to subtle health issues: occasional discomfort, chronic fatigue often ignored. 'Small regrets' warns if neglected, minor issues may worsen but no major harm. Bo to Gen: Bo is consumption, Gen is stillness; rest and preserve energy. Health direction: prevention, pay attention to subtle signs like sleep, digestion. Do: regular check-ups, consult early for small symptoms; gentle exercise (tai chi, walking). Avoid: ignoring symptoms, overworking. Mentally, 'laugh heartily' advises optimistic, broad-minded outlook to support health.
study: In study, 'jade and stone still have flaws' suggests knowledge gaps or careless exam mistakes. 'Small regrets' advises not to let small errors depress you. 'In the end no blame' predicts overall stable performance, not failing. Bo to Gen: Bo is decline, Gen is stillness; strengthen basics, don't aim too high. Specific: review errors, refine weak points like polishing jade. Exam luck acceptable but careful reading needed to avoid 'small regrets.' Do: plan to fill gaps, focus on prone-to-error types; adjust mindset before exam. Avoid: obsessing over obscure topics while ignoring foundation, or panicking after one mistake.
general: The sign teaches: worldly affairs like jade have flaws, small regrets are inevitable, but the end is harmless—be at ease. Bo to Gen advises stopping loss and keeping correct. Action: 1. check everything meticulously to nip problems in bud; 2. when small losses occur, cut them off—don't let regret amplify; 3. be open-minded, smile through waves, trust 'no blame' omen. Mentally, imperfection is normal; flaws in jade don't dim its light. Accepting flaws allows moving forward calmly. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
Drooping wings, flying far to the sky,
All to flee from harm's way.
Along this path, a worldly aim held high,
Again the garden fills with spring's display.
interpretation1: The sign begins with 'drooping wings, flying far to the sky,' likening a bird with lowered wings flying a distant journey—wings drooping suggest lack of strength, and the distant sky implies a long road, hinting at blocked fortune and a need to temporarily hide one's edge and avoid turmoil. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' turns the meaning: even while retreating, one still harbors aspirations to benefit the world, or may find new opportunities in ordinary livelihood. 'Again the garden fills with spring's display' ends with brightness, declaring that hardship will eventually pass and vitality will return. This sign belongs to Kan Palace, with original hexagram Ming Yi (Darkening of the Light) transforming into Qian (Modesty). Ming Yi's image is the sun entering the earth, light being wounded, matching the 'fleeing from harm' moment, requiring perseverance and self-preservation; Qian hexagram shows a mountain buried in the earth, humbling oneself, ultimately leading to success. The two hexagrams together indicate first experiencing darkness, then turning to good fortune through modesty—retreat is not defeat, but gathering strength for the right time. The entire sign suggests: in adversity, protect yourself wisely, stay true to your original intent, and keep your feet on the ground; then you will naturally see the clouds part and the moon appear, and spring will return to your courtyard.
career: 'Drooping wings, flying far to the sky' suggests your career may suffer external setbacks, such as being sidelined or projects blocked. Follow the bird's example: fold your wings and avoid the storm for now, don't force a fight. 'Fleeing from harm's way' is not passive escape but retreating to advance—adjust your position or work behind the scenes, then slowly plan your comeback. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' indicates you can still achieve while lying low; focus on deepening your expertise and accumulating resources, or shift to a more practical role. 'Garden fills with spring' signals a turning point; next spring is especially favorable for a comeback or opening a new chapter. Kan Palace, Ming Yi to Qian: Kun is receptive, Dui is joyful. At this time, avoid confrontations; be modest and inclusive, help colleagues, and you'll build a good reputation instead. Advice: proactively apply for long-term projects that require patient cultivation; choose your alliances carefully. Avoid: openly challenging authority or making hasty job changes.
wealth: 'Drooping wings' means your wings are not yet fully fledged—regular income may decrease, and you may easily incur losses due to various matters. 'Fleeing from harm's way' calls for conservative risk avoidance—guard against investment traps and guarantee disputes. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' alludes to self-reliance; wealth comes from diligent work, small businesses, or skills—speculative gains are unlikely. 'Again the garden fills with spring' suggests that after this period, finances may slowly recover. Ming Yi hexagram has the trouble of brightness being injured—beware of losses from partnerships; the transformation to Qian, a mountain within the earth, suggests solid and restrained wealth management. Lean toward defense, not offense. Advice: hoard cash, cut unnecessary expenses, deepen your main business. Avoid: following speculative trends or engaging in private lending. Favorable industries: land, food, education, and other stable sectors.
love: A bird with drooping wings traveling alone mirrors feelings of loneliness, distance, or the silent treatment in relationships. 'Fleeing from harm's way' suggests temporary separation, possibly due to external pressure or misunderstandings—a deliberate distance to preserve the relationship. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' reminds that during this pause, you can reflect on how to manage the relationship, cultivate your heart, and reshape yourself. 'Garden fills with spring' finally brings warmth—the ordeal makes the bond stronger. Ming Yi's darkness turns into Qian's harmony; resolve resentment with gentleness and modesty; take the initiative to step back, leaving room for turning around. Singles: past wounds are slowly healing; don't rush to find a partner; still waters run deep, and fate will come when you're ready. Those with partners: avoid dredging up old grievances; break the ice gently through letters or sharing daily life; wait for things to naturally fall into place.
health: Drooping wings suggest fatigue and lack of strength—beware of overwork leading to illness or recurrence of old ailments, especially cardiovascular issues and eye strain (Ming Yi original hexagram involves Li fire being injured). 'Fleeing from harm's way' is actually a warning from your body to rest—reduce your load, avoid overexertion. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' subtly points to the way of health: daily routines, proper diet, and orderly living accumulate energy. 'Garden fills with spring' indicates that with proper care, vitality can be restored. Kan Palace governs kidneys and ears; moderate sexual activity, avoid staying up late, listen to soothing music. Advice: gentle morning walks, eat black rice and mulberries to nourish kidneys. Avoid: strenuous exercise or ignoring symptoms.
study: 'Drooping wings' like a tired bird—academic progress may hit a bottleneck, grades may temporarily dip, or exams may not go as expected. 'Fleeing from harm's way' advises temporarily avoiding cutthroat competition; adjust your goals or pivot to a secondary area—retreat to advance. 'Along this path, a worldly aim held high' ties to 'worldly aim'—studies require persistent dedication, accumulating step by step, to break through. 'Garden fills with spring' finally brings a turn in exam luck, or you may top the spring exams. Ming Yi to Qian: be humble in asking teachers for guidance; don't be ashamed to ask. Beneficial subjects: literature, history, policy essays—those requiring deep reflection. Avoid: taking shortcuts or cramming at the last minute.
general: This sign starts dim and ends bright, like a phoenix reborn. The key lies in staying true to your ambition while 'taking refuge,' and moving steadily on the 'worldly path.' Those in difficulty should know: drooping wings are not forever; the distant sky is reachable. Those in good times, hearing of the garden full of spring, should also remember the hard journey. Actions: 1) Immediately review your relationships and finances—cut losses decisively; 2) Read and meditate daily to cultivate modesty; 3) Seek opportunities in small details, don't rush for quick success. Keep spring in your heart, and don't fear the floating clouds; you will turn danger into safety.

sign_text:
Good tidings arrive,
Bringing a smile so bright.
Soaring once, leaving all behind,
Parting the clouds, ascending to the sky.
interpretation1: The four lines flow in spirit, rising from earth to heaven, echoing the hexagram change from Sheng (Ascending) to Ding (Cauldron) in Zhen Palace. The first line 'Good tidings arrive, bringing a smile so bright' depicts a letter arriving at the door, a joyful expression—a metaphor for sudden opportunity that melts stagnation. Sheng hexagram: Xun wood enters Kun earth, like a seed breaking ground; 'good tidings' is the sprouting trigger. Ding hexagram: Li fire burns Xun wood, meaning discarding the old and embracing the new; the smile is the fire illuminating the heart. The second line 'Soaring once, leaving all behind, parting the clouds, ascending to the sky' evokes a hawk breaking its tether, soaring upward—clouds part, the sun appears, the heavenly path opens. Zhen governs movement; Sheng accumulates small to become great; the transformation to Ding symbolizes forging into a vessel, meaning earthly troubles eventually become stairs, and with favorable wind one can reach the azure clouds. The entire sign uses 'tidings' as a lead, 'smile' as a turn, 'soaring' as momentum, and 'sky' as destination—four images interlocking, showing that in favorable times, one must hold the virtue of Sheng: the great image of Sheng says 'the superior person, following virtue, accumulates small things to become great'; one should not forget the foundation in haste. The great image of Ding says 'the superior person rectifies position and consolidates destiny'; after achievement, be steady and guard success. This is a very favorable sign; the heavenly mechanism is already moving, but it awaits the right person to respond.
career: The sign's 'good tidings arrive' clearly points to a definite happy event in your career—such as a transfer order, appointment, project approval—coming like a sudden spring thunder. 'Parting the clouds, ascending to the sky' is especially favorable for a climb in position. Zhen palace is active, Sheng hexagram indicates stepping up, Ding hexagram discards the old, all suggesting job changes and increased responsibilities. Your current career is like climbing a ladder of clouds—take the initiative to take on tasks that require cross-departmental coordination, public speaking, or innovative breakthroughs. It's especially beneficial for management, technology, education, and training roles that need insight to 'part the clouds.' Zhen palace wood energy is strong; spring and morning hours bring many opportunities. However, 'soaring once, leaving all behind' implies a risk of losing roots—beware of getting dizzy with success or detaching from the team. Ding hexagram cooks food, meaning achievements require collective effort; gather people's hearts before taking action. Advice: move when you hear the news, but gather advice before acting. Avoid: abandoning old allies as soon as you gain power.
wealth: tech-related non-traditional income
love: 'Good tidings arrive, bringing a smile so bright' perfectly describes the joy of a good match—singles suddenly hear from a matchmaker or an old friend rekindles feelings, sweeping away loneliness. Sheng hexagram: two trees leaning on each other—relationships deepening step by step. Ding hexagram: Li fire below, Xun wind above, cooking and blending—passionate yet harmonious feelings. For those with partners, 'soaring once, leaving all behind' indicates shared growth and advancement—like moving together or starting a business—the relationship strengthens due to shared goals. But beware: after 'parting the clouds and ascending to the sky,' your vision may become lofty, leading to contempt. In interaction, be upright like the cauldron, honest like cooking fresh food, and frequently send 'good tidings'—greetings, small gifts, shared memories. Avoid: neglecting your partner's feelings in pride, or rushing to publicize the relationship, which may cause gossip. Late spring to early summer is the peak time for romantic luck.
health: The sign's clear sky imagery—'parting the clouds, ascending to the sky'—suggests smooth qi and blood, dissolution of stagnation. Zhen palace belongs to wood and corresponds to the liver; Sheng hexagram: Xun wind enters Kun earth—guard against spleen dampness and wood depression. Ding hexagram: Li fire warms the middle, beneficial for warming and unblocking. Recently, your spirit may feel renewed; old ailments like morning fog meeting the sun. For health, imitate 'Zhen's movement': gentle morning walks, stretching. Diet imitate 'Ding's harmony': increase pungent-warm foods, reduce sticky greasy ones, to soothe liver qi. However, 'soaring once, leaving all behind' warns against overwork depleting your essence—when the cauldron is used to its extreme, it overturns. Keep regular hours, avoid staying up late and excessive drinking. Subtle sign: occasional fiery eyes or light sleep—this is due to rising yang too quickly; drink chrysanthemum and goji tea to calm it. The foundation of health lies in a smiling heart; so regulating the spirit comes first.
study: 'Good tidings arrive' most corresponds to exam results, admission notices, or thesis approvals—happy academic news. 'Parting the clouds, ascending to the sky' symbolizes a sudden breakthrough in understanding—previously puzzling points are suddenly clear, like sunlight piercing through clouds. Zhen palace governs active thinking; Sheng hexagram accumulates small to become large. Use the 'Ding's innovation' method: integrate and restructure old knowledge into a system, not rote memorization. Beneficial for abstract subjects like science, philosophy, and metaphysics—both arts and sciences can 'soar.' Exam luck is good, but Ding hexagram shows 'cooking' requires proper heat; preparation should include mock tests and repeated refinement. Avoid: being cocky and underestimating the test, or rushing on the day. For interviews and defenses, dress neatly like the dignity of a cauldron; speak clearly like skies clearing—this will boost your luck.
general: When you rise, don't forget to nurture your roots—remember those who helped you...

sign_text:
The blue felt rug is kept, but empty and old.
On the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold.
Do not be fooled by a moment's delight.
Consider well: this sign may be ominous tonight.
interpretation1: The sign's 'the blue felt rug is kept, but empty and old' alludes to the story of Wang Xianzhi in the Book of Jin—the blue felt was a family heirloom, here hinting at stubbornly clinging to old ways, holding onto decay; the word 'empty' indicates futility—if you simply follow the old path, you'll gain nothing. 'On the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold' uses the image of a precarious nest atop a tree swaying in the wind to warn that the foundation is already unstable, and now external turbulence adds risk. Wind is the image of Xun, governing penetration and impermanence, suggesting change is constant. The last two lines 'Do not be fooled by a moment's delight; consider well: this sign may be ominous tonight' directly address the heart: don't be blinded by small immediate gains; be wary of the hidden danger beneath. The hexagram: Kan Palace, Shi (Army) transforming into Jie (Deliverance). Shi hexagram: Kun above, Kan below—acting dangerously yet smoothly, implying conflict and worry. The transformation to Jie: Zhen above, Kan below—thunder and rain bring relief. However, Kan represents repeated pitfalls; Shi to Jie seems to indicate obstacles are dissolving, but the first line 'no blame' only brings safety, while the top line still warns of 'shooting the hawk,' indicating residual trouble lingers. Combining sign and hexagram, this sign is mixed: calm on the surface, undercurrents beneath—be cautious and vigilant, don't let small peace make you forget great danger.
career: 'The blue felt rug is kept, but empty and old' warns that if you stubbornly stick to your old position without progress, you may waste time. 'On the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold' hints that your team or project has an unstable foundation; personnel changes or external fluctuations are imminent. The sign says 'do not be fooled by a moment's delight'—if you get a temporary promotion or small win, don't be complacent; greater challenges may lurk behind. 'Consider well: this sign may be ominous tonight' urges you to prepare for a rainy day. Shi hexagram emphasizes discipline; Jie hexagram focuses on relief. In career, break the deadlock through pragmatism and change. Review your weaknesses, actively expand skills—especially beneficial to reach outward, engage in cross-department collaboration, or transformative innovation. In timing, when the wind rises, it's exactly the chance to gain experience. Avoid: seeking comfort and being perfunctory; avoid competing for superficial credit while ignoring hidden conflicts.
wealth: 'Blue felt rug' represents your base or fixed assets; keeping the old way will leave your wealth source barren—if you only rely on old savings or a single income stream, it's hard to grow. 'On the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold' directly points to financial foundation vulnerable to disturbance—like investment volatility or unexpected expenses—guard against risk. 'Do not be fooled by a moment's delight' targets speculative wealth: if you make a small profit from speculation, don't get smug; losses may follow. Jie hexagram has a meaning of relief, but Kan water is dangerous; manage wealth defensively, not offensively. For regular income, focus on your main job—don't lightly change jobs for a raise. For speculative wealth and investments, stay conservative and observe—avoid high-risk areas, especially projects with high volatility. Avoid: blindly expanding credit, guaranteeing for others, or increasing debt. Focus on cash reserves to prepare for contingencies.
love: 'The blue felt rug is kept, but empty and old' in relationships points to habitual maintenance—like an old rug, familiar but lacking novelty; if supported only by habit, it feels empty. 'Nest on the branch' is the love nest, 'wind' implies external disturbance or inner wavering—hinting at hidden turmoil, perhaps from misunderstandings or outside temptations. 'Do not be fooled by a moment's delight' warns against indulging in fleeting warmth—like making up after a quarrel superficially without addressing root causes. Singles: don't jump into a relationship based on a brief spark. Shi hexagram often involves disputes; Jie hexagram advocates reconciliation. In interaction, communicate honestly, actively repair cracks, rather than just muddle through. Advice: arrange shared experiences to rekindle passion. Avoid: bringing up old grievances, sulking, or suppressing true feelings for surface peace.
health: The sign's 'blue felt rug' represents old chronic conditions or long-standing habits; 'kept but empty and old' suggests that if you remain lazy, your health will hardly improve. Kan palace corresponds to kidneys and ears; Shi hexagram indicates fatigue and depletion; Jie hexagram offers relief, but 'on the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold'—the 'wind' image points to external pathogenic factors—guard against seasonal flu, allergies, and emotional fluctuations that may trigger old ailments. This sign reminds you that temporary recovery from minor ailments should not be taken as 'a moment's delight'—relapse may lurk. For health regimen: follow the seasons—keep your lower back and kidneys warm, eat light and diuretic foods, adjust your schedule to consolidate your foundation. Choose gentle exercises like Tai Chi or walking; avoid heavy sweating and wind. Pay attention to sleep disorders and unexplained fatigue, adjust promptly, don't let overwork lead to illness.
study: 'The blue felt rug is kept, but empty and old' directly points to outdated learning methods—clinging to outdated textbooks and relying on rote memorization—hard to adapt to new question changes, yielding little gain. 'On the branch, the nest feels the wind's cold' suggests weak points in your knowledge system that may be exposed in big exams. The sign says 'do not be fooled by a moment's delight'—occasional high scores should not make you arrogant; your actual foundation is unstable. 'Consider well: this sign may be ominous tonight' urges you to examine your weaknesses and reinforce them early. Shi hexagram values discipline; Jie hexagram values thorough understanding. For studies, systematically organize your knowledge, delve into principles—especially beneficial for science and logic subjects; for arts, emphasize integration. Exam luck is average—prepare early, adapt to the exam environment, and have a solid strategy. Avoid: mindless question practice without understanding, last-minute cramming, or underestimating the challenge.
general: This sign uses the imagery of keeping old ways leading to emptiness, and a precarious nest in wind, pointing to a mix of good and bad. Seemingly stable, but secretly unstable—momentary joy must be doubly guarded. The core is to turn from defense to offense, adapt to change. Primary actions: 1) Thoroughly examine the foundation of your current situation—patch up weaknesses in career, finance, and relationships one by one. 2) Abandon wishful thinking—don't rush in because of small gains; stay righteous and wait for the right time. 3) Maintain a sense of urgency but don't fall into anxiety; like Jie hexagram says, after thunder and rain, stretch out calmly. Mentally, remember 'do not be fooled by a moment's delight'—always hold reverence even when the wind is calm, so you can see through undercurrents and turn danger into safety.

sign_text:
Do not say the thorns are harsh and grim.
In the end, the phoenix will perch on them.
For now, there is a time to wait and see.
Why worry early? Let your heart be free.
interpretation1: The sign poem uses thorns and the phoenix as metaphors. Opening with 'Do not say the thorns are harsh and grim' advises not to complain about the brambles and harsh environment before you. 'In the end, the phoenix will perch on them' reveals that this thorny thicket will eventually become a resting place for the phoenix—adversity is actually the backdrop for auspiciousness. 'For now, there is a time to wait and see' directly states that now you must patiently bide your time, not rush. 'Why worry early? Let your heart be free' comforts you not to waver prematurely, wasting energy. The hexagrams belong to Gen Palace, Sun (Decrease) transforming into Zhong Fu (Inner Truth). Gen is mountain, governing stillness and gathering momentum. The Sun hexagram text 'decrease below to increase above' teaches reducing inflated energy and excessive aggression—retreat to advance. The changed hexagram Zhong Fu: Xun above, Dui below—wind and lake resonate, symbolizing sincerity connecting. The entire hexagram reveals: though it appears as a unfavorable sign now, with thorns blocking the way, heaven rewards sincerity; if you can hold to stillness and patience, the thorny thicket will naturally give rise to the phoenix's grace. The core of this sign lies in the word 'wait'—keep a low profile and cultivate, do not be deceived by appearances.
career: The 'thorns harsh and grim' directly describes a career landscape full of prickles—perhaps petty people obstructing, project dangers, promotion stagnation. 'In the end, the phoenix will perch on them' shows that this difficulty is actually the foundation for achievement. Sun hexagram emphasizes decrease—now is the time to voluntarily give up non-core interests or yield the spotlight, avoiding direct confrontation with superiors or colleagues. Zhong Fu stresses treating people with sincerity and establishing trust through honesty. The time is not yet ripe, as 'for now, there is a time to wait'—not suitable for job hopping, changing industries, or aggressively seeking merit. Focus on honing your professional skills, engage in behind-the-scenes planning, data analysis, and other quiet waiting work; there may be a turn in autumn or winter. Avoid: public complaints, factional fighting—guard against losing reputation through speech. 'Why worry early?' precisely reminds you not to panic and mess up your own rhythm in anxiety.
wealth: 'Thorns harsh and grim' describes a rough path to wealth—seeking money is like cutting through brambles, easy to incur losses. 'Phoenix will perch' suggests that in the long run, this thorny ground can actually build a nest to attract the phoenix—opportunities hidden in difficulty. Sun hexagram clearly indicates now is the time to reduce wasteful spending and defend what you have. Zhong Fu emphasizes that integrity brings cooperation—avoid speculative shortcuts. Wealth leans toward regular income; manage finances conservatively, cut unnecessary expenses, don't expand investments or venture into marginal fields. If you are in earth-related industries (real estate, agriculture) or metal industries, there may be small gains within the year, but you need to wait patiently. 'Why worry early?' advises not to be anxious about temporary scarcity and rush into risky moves. During this period, you can study financial skills and wait for the next spring when wood is strong (wood overcomes earth, loosening Gen palace's restraint) before planning development.
love: Sun hexagram advises reducing stubbornness and thinking more for the other person.
health: 'Thorns harsh and grim' suggests your health may be troubled by minor ailments, like thorns pricking your skin, causing discomfort. 'Phoenix will perch' indicates that these ailments will eventually heal, and you may even become more aware of self-care because of them. Gen is mountain, belonging to earth; the hexagram governs the spleen and stomach. Sun hexagram advises reducing dietary burden—avoid raw, cold, and greasy foods; eat smaller, more frequent meals. The changed hexagram Zhong Fu: upper Xun is wind, governing breathing; lower Dui is mouth—pay attention to throat and lung health, prevent wind-pathogen invasion. Currently, 'there is a time to wait'—recovery requires patient rest, not rushing to take tonics or overexert yourself. Mentally, 'why worry early?'—don't be overly anxious about minor illnesses. Maintain the peaceful sincerity of Zhong Fu, with moderate gentle movement (Gen does not mean complete stillness), and you will gradually restore vitality.
study: 'Thorns harsh and grim' depicts encountering difficult subjects or bottlenecks in studies, like walking through a thicket—arduous progress. 'In the end, the phoenix will perch on them' says this tempering will eventually be the foundation of achievement—the thorns are the training ground for the phoenix learning to fly. Sun hexagram advises reducing distractions—focus on one subject, avoid dividing attention. Zhong Fu emphasizes seeking truth earnestly—avoid academic shortcuts. Current grades may be unsatisfactory for now; 'there is a time to wait' indicates waiting for the right moment to mature—major examinations require full preparation before participating, not rushing in. In learning methods, adopt Gen's 'stopping' approach: set a fixed time each day for quiet study, reduce frivolous socializing. When winter water is strong, academics may see a breakthrough. Favorable for arts, history, philosophy, and other subjects requiring quiet contemplation. Avoid: being impatient, giving up halfway.
general: This sign, with the imagery of thorns and the phoenix, reveals the principle that 'adversity ultimately becomes a field of blessings.'
