The entire hexagram's eye is the word 'wait': Sun transforming into Zhong Fu teaches reducing impatience for quick success and guarding with sincere heart, awaiting heaven's timing. Three action suggestions: First, hold 'Gen still'—suspend major decisions like investments, job changes, or relationship changes; stabilize first before seeking change. Second, practice 'Sun reduction'—cut back on unnecessary expenses, socializing, and distracting thoughts; conserve energy. Third, cultivate 'Zhong Fu'—treat everyone with genuine sincerity; this is the sure way to sense good fortune. Mentally, no need to 'worry early'—don't let anxiety consume your spirit. Thorns are only temporary; the phoenix's arrival is not far off. After the wind and frost, there will surely be a clear song to report.

sign_text:
Above and below in harmony,
Sorrow and worry resolved.
A thousand peaks of clouds,
One single round moon.
interpretation1: The sign text 'above and below in harmony, sorrow and worry resolved' seems to suggest harmony and ease, but Kui hexagram is inherently about estrangement, and its transformation into Wei Ji (Before Completion) indicates that this harmony is only a temporary calm. The Gen Palace signifies stopping, with mountains blocking the way, matching the 'thousand peaks of clouds'—numerous obstacles and dense mist obscuring insight in career, relationships, and health. The 'one round moon' is a bright hope, but it is hidden by clouds and peaks, visible but unreachable, like a mirage. This sign warns: luck is adverse, do not be complacent with temporary peace; look beneath the surface, beware of recurring conflicts, and guard against failure after initial success.
career: 'Above and below in harmony' may indicate a temporary calm at work, but Kui transforming into Wei Ji suggests hidden disagreements within the team, making collaboration unsustainable, even risking division. 'Thousand peaks of clouds' directly points to layers of obstacles on the promotion path; even if a 'single round moon' opportunity appears, it is elusive and fraught with difficulties. The Gen hexagram indicates stopping; it is better to stay still than to act rashly. At this stage, focus on your current duties, deepen your skills, and avoid initiating job changes or starting a business. If facing competition or project bidding, first investigate internal alignment and avoid contending with colleagues for credit. The timing may be around autumn, but it will not be quick; proceed slowly.
wealth: 'Thousand peaks of clouds' obscures the view; income paths are also full of stumbling blocks.
love: 'Above and below in harmony' reflects a superficial calm in relationships; past 'sorrows' may be temporarily relieved, but Kui transforming into Wei Ji suggests unhealed cracks and recurring suspicion. 'Thousand peaks of clouds' blocks genuine connection; partners may have many invisible barriers. The 'single round moon' of affection remains distant from reality. For singles, someone met may seem compatible at first but soon reveal differences; do not rush into commitment just to avoid being alone. For those in a relationship, honest communication is needed to clear the air; avoid bringing up old grievances or touching sensitive spots. Marriage issues may be stalled; time is needed to melt the ice. The ultimate hexagram is Wei Ji (not yet completed), so do not force immediate harmony.
health: 'Above and below in harmony' may refer to a temporary balance of yin and yang, but Kui transforming into Wei Ji suggests that the qi mechanism is prone to disorder, and old worries lurk beneath. 'Thousand peaks of clouds' hints at spleen and stomach imbalances. Gen is mountain, belonging to Earth; especially guard against digestive disorders. 'One round moon' corresponds to the head; overuse of mental energy may cause fatigue. Rest and diet regulation are key: eat small meals frequently to protect the spleen, take morning and evening walks to soothe liver qi. Rest when needed, avoid agitation and rash action.
study: Exam luck is fraught with difficulties; test anxiety or misreading may cause point loss.
general: The way of this unfavorable sign lies in perceiving the subtle beneath the surface: apparent 'harmony' and 'resolution' cannot be fully trusted. Only by constant vigilance can one overcome the 'thousand peaks of clouds.' 1) Deeply investigate personal and professional matters, eliminate hidden gaps, do not let small issues become chronic. 2) Prepare multiple strategies, always leave room, so as not to be caught off guard by changes. 3) Guard your heart like the 'single round moon' — though clouds and fog obscure, do not lose your clear resolve. When luck is rough, retreat and mend nets, wait for dawn; that is far better than blind action. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

sign_text:
Jade emerges from Kunlun's stone,
The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal.
Coming and going at last has hope;
To act or hide, there is no difficulty.
interpretation1: The sign text 'Jade emerges from Kunlun's stone' uses the image of fine jade hidden in stone, indicating that precious things only shine after polishing, suggesting the seeker has talent and virtue, only waiting for the right moment. 'The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal' depicts a light boat leaving the shallows of an old ferry, symbolizing an end to stagnation and a clear path ahead, a new journey beginning. 'Coming and going at last has hope; use or set aside, no difficulty' is a play on Confucius' saying, indicating whether advancing or retreating, there is hope; success or disappointment can be faced with equanimity. The hexagram is Dui Palace, Dui transforming into Sui: Dui is joy, eloquence; Sui is following, adapting to the times. Dui becoming Sui means joy turns into compliance, suggesting adapting to circumstances with gentle joy, not going against the natural order. Overall, this is a very favorable sign, implying that past accumulation is sufficient; it is time to emerge like jade from stone or a boat from the shoal; acting in harmony with the trend brings success, but one must remain composed, avoiding arrogance and impatience, to match the hexagram virtue.
career: Career is like 'jade emerging from Kunlun's stone' — talents are about to be discovered, standing out from ordinary positions or cluttered tasks. 'The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal' directly points to career changes, such as position adjustments, new project launches, or job-hopping opportunities; stagnation ends. Dui hexagram governs communication; Sui governs teamwork. Therefore, actively express opinions at work, take on tasks that showcase abilities, such as bidding, reporting, external liaison. Favorable: pay attention to opportunities in autumn or soon, volunteer proactively, gain recognition by demonstrating expertise. Avoid: contending with colleagues for credit or being overly flashy after success. Like 'use or set aside, no difficulty,' advance or retreat with measure; even if temporarily sidelined, remain principled. Sui hexagram reminds 'the gentleman goes to rest at dusk,' so keep a regular schedule amidst busyness to stay energized.
wealth: Dui is metal, governs joyful wealth, such as cooperation dividends, income derived from persuasive skills; Sui hexagram does not favor aggression, so speculative gains are moderate.
love: 'Jade emerges from Kunlun's stone' suggests inner beauty gradually reveals, or a partner may seem ordinary but has potential worth discovering. 'The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal' indicates that the relationship moves out of stagnation into a new stage, such as defining the relationship or ending long-distance. Dui is joy, pleasant communication; Sui suggests go with the flow. Singles may meet a good match in social settings; the person may not be flashy but reveals character over time; take the initiative to show kindness, avoid impatience or forcing. Couples can deepen their bond, discuss the future (the boat leaves the shore); when small conflicts arise, learn to yield, 'use or set aside, no difficulty' — not fixated on who is right, but mutually tolerant. Avoid bringing up past grievances or being controlling; handle with ease, and love deepens naturally.
health: 'Jade emerges from Kunlun's stone' suggests that latent physical discomfort may surface, which is just a good opportunity for treatment; minor ailments exposed can be cured. 'The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal' symbolizes breaking free from sub-health inertia, starting new health habits. Dui hexagram corresponds to mouth, lungs, throat; beware of respiratory, skin, and oral issues, especially in autumn. Sui hexagram emphasizes following nature's rhythm, so early to bed and early to rise, morning deep breathing or jogging, light moistening diet such as pears, lily bulb. Watch for recurrent cough, dry throat, skin allergies; avoid staying up late, excessive worry. The way of health is to keep 'coming and going' orderly, balance work and rest, not be troubled by minor ailments.
study: Studies are like 'jade emerging from Kunlun's stone' — not achieved quickly; require continuous polishing, especially at breakthrough points like chipping stone for jade; after patience, clarity dawns. 'The boat leaves the ancient ferry's shoal' symbolizes leaving learning bottlenecks; methods or directions may need adjustment; a new plan sets sail. Dui hexagram favors oral exams, language, arts; Sui hexagram favors learning with teachers and friends. Exam luck is good; maintain a calm mind ('use or set aside, no difficulty'), arrive early, prepare supplies. Favorable: form study groups, quiz each other to reinforce memory. Avoid: last-minute cramming, worrying about results. The timing is often favorable in autumn; exams will have steady performance with highlights.
general: This is a very favorable sign; the core is that the time has come; act in accordance with the trend. Past accumulation is like jade in stone; now is the moment to emerge. Stalled matters are like a boat stuck on a shoal; they will soon set sail. Key action suggestions: 1) Examine your strengths, like 'jade' awaiting polishing, actively showcase your talents to the outside world. 2) Resolutely break from old patterns, like 'the boat leaves the ancient ferry,' push forward delayed plans or change environments. 3) Understand the principle of 'coming and going, use or set aside' — there is room whether advancing or retreating; do not waver due to temporary gain or loss. In mindset, be like a boat on a calm lake, serene and unhurried; eliminate agitation and attachment, and you will achieve a good outcome. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
At present, the mind finds it hard to relax.
A guest comes slowly, slowly.
Though the golden carriage endures danger,
Regret will have an end at last.
interpretation1: The first line 'At present, the mind finds it hard to relax' directly points to a troubled, anxious state. 'A guest comes slowly, slowly' — this 'guest' is not a swift rescuer but a slow-arriving opportunity or late benefactor, coming unhurriedly. 'Though the golden carriage endures danger' — the golden carriage is a precious vehicle, yet it travels a perilous road, indicating that the situation is dangerous, but the foundation remains; the road ahead is rough. 'Regret will have an end at last' — 'regret' means difficulty and regret, but 'end at last' reveals that this hardship will eventually end; it is not an eternal night without dawn. The hexagram is Kun (Distress) transforming into Kan (Water). Kun is above Dui, below Kan: lake without water, symbolizing distress; becoming Kan, water, double peril, warning against rash action, advising quiet waiting. However, water also represents wisdom and flow; extreme danger brings change; respond with flexibility. The core of this sign is 'slow' and 'wait'; do not contend for the moment, and the hidden obstacles will dissipate.
career: The 'golden carriage' metaphorically represents one's professional status or project; though valuable, it is in danger, indicating current career bottlenecks such as blocked plans, fierce competition, or resource shortages. 'A guest comes slowly, slowly' suggests that the turnaround comes from external assistance or a new collaboration, but requires patience; do not push. Kun hexagram: Dui is mouth, Kan is pit; it is advisable to speak cautiously and act more than argue. Currently, avoid forcing a breakthrough, or you may suffer from pride in the 'golden carriage.' The hexagram transforms into Kan Water; water flows downward, so it is wise to stay low-key temporarily, solidify foundations, and engage in behind-the-scenes, preparatory, or research work. If considering a job transfer or job change, this is not a good time; wait quietly until autumn or winter, when the 'guest' may arrive and the situation gradually opens.
wealth: Dui palace is metal, Kun transforming into Kan water depletes the metal element's energy, suggesting loss.
love: 'At present, the mind finds it hard to relax' describes a troubled state in a relationship, such as cold war, misunderstanding, or physical distance. 'A guest comes slowly, slowly' for singles suggests a new connection that is ambiguous, with the other party being lukewarm; it will take time to test, so do not rush to define the relationship. For those in a relationship, 'though the golden carriage endures danger' signifies a strong bond but facing difficulties together; do not blame each other. Kun hexagram: Dui is a young girl, Kan is a middle-aged man; yin and yang do not connect, easily leading to estrangement; use softness to overcome hardness, listen more, blame less. 'Regret will have an end at last' indicates that as long as you don't give up easily, after the storm passes, feelings will be more stable. Avoid bringing up old grievances or forcing promises.
health: Kun hexagram: upper Dui is mouth, lower Kan is ear and kidney; the transformed double Kan indicates excessive water, suggesting kidney qi and urinary system fatigue, or emotional low leading to psychosomatic disorders. 'Mind hard to relax' refers to unresolved emotions that over time damage liver qi, causing dry eyes and migraines. 'A guest comes slowly, slowly' implies slow recovery; health maintenance requires persistence, not quick results. Focus on nourishing kidney water: eat light foods, more black beans, black sesame; meditate to calm the mind. The hexagram changing to Kan signals danger of accidents, especially involving water or heights. Best to take slow morning walks to soothe qi; especially keep warm in winter.
study: 'At present, the mind finds it hard to relax' is like a student sitting at a desk with scattered thoughts, unable to concentrate or remember. 'Though the golden carriage endures danger' suggests that the student's ability is good, but external interference or wrong methods create a bottleneck. 'A guest comes slowly, slowly' hints that a wise teacher or helpful friend will come slowly to resolve doubts, but the student must actively seek help, not wait idly. Kan hexagram represents wisdom; when distress reaches its extreme, change occurs. At this time, adjust strategies, focus on weak points, avoid spreading too thin. Exam luck is delayed; prepare well in advance; on the day, details may cause point loss, so double-check. When the 'guest' arrives, clarity will come; do not deny yourself due to temporary setbacks.
general: This sign is like clouds covering the moon — not an eternal night, but a time before dawn. The core lies in the word 'slowly' — everything resolves when taken slowly. Key action suggestions: 1) When encountering obstacles, pause actions, review available resources (the 'golden carriage'), and focus on protecting core interests. 2) Wait quietly for external help or timing (the 'guest'); during the wait, engage in study, organization, and planning; do not panic. 3) Believe that 'regret will have an end at last'; know that difficulties have a limit; keep your heart calm; do not add new trouble through agitation. In mindset, be like still water reflecting objects — though in danger, remain clear; then the turning point quietly arises. Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment.

sign_text:
It can be entrusted;
It can be committed.
Matters are slow,
But there is no mistake.
interpretation1: The sign text 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' indicates that plans can be entrusted to others or left to time; it has the image of trust and delivery. 'Matters are slow' is not an obstacle but a gestation before natural success, like spring planting awaiting autumn. 'There is no mistake' is the ultimate guarantee; even if there are twists, they will not damage the foundation. The hexagram belongs to Xun Palace, Gu transforming into Ding. Gu (Mountain Wind) is upper Gen, lower Xun: wind enters below the mountain, accumulated decay needing correction, indicating that the old situation is rotten and must be cleansed. Ding (Fire Wind) is upper Li, lower Xun: wood feeds fire, cooking food and nurturing talents, signifying establishing new foundations and a fresh start. Gu transforming into Ding means first removing poison, then feasting, which matches the sign's rhythm: at first it seems slow because clearing decay takes time; ultimately there will be no mistake because the Ding stands firm. It aligns with the virtue of 'Xun compliance' — gradual progress through gentleness brings good fortune. This very favorable sign does not promise quick joy but rather steady achievement through delayed effort; it is advisable to trust others and stick to one's principles, avoiding impatience for quick results.
career: The sign 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' directly points to career: there are trustworthy people to delegate tasks or important responsibilities to be shouldered. There may be opportunities for project assignment or position transition. 'Matters are slow' reminds that promotion or achievement manifestation may be delayed; not due to lack of talent, but because the situation needs to coalesce. The Gu transforming into Ding hexagram: Gu is old system and decay; Ding is new rules and regulations. Therefore, it is favorable to actively participate in internal rectification, process optimization, or team restructuring; this is a window to build reputation and trust. Xun governs entering; approach details with a gentle and flexible attitude, avoiding flaunting credit. Suitable for coordination, auditing, and administrative roles; turning points likely in autumn and winter. Specifically: take on leftover problems to show responsibility; avoid giving conflicting orders or breaking promises lightly. Timing may be slow, but 'no mistake' guarantees that solid effort will be properly rewarded.
wealth: 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' regarding wealth means that funds can be entrusted to solid, long-term assets, not for quick flips. 'Matters are slow' clearly indicates a longer return cycle; do not rush for gains in a single year or season. Xun Palace Gu transforming into Ding: Gu is decay, Ding is cooking; first clear old debts, organize messy accounts, then establish a new financial structure. Avoid gullibly following speculative tips; guard against the seduction of 'Gu.' Wealth trend is a 'slow-paced bull market': stable regular income, occasional but moderate speculative gains. It is advisable to invest funds in batches into reputable platforms or real estate; avoid chasing short-term high returns or making oral agreements in partnerships. Specific actions: entrust management to professionals; avoid frequent operations; favor culture, education, food (Ding image) industries; like hoarding wealth in a tripod, waiting for the right time to mature.
love: The sign 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' is a good omen for emotional sincerity; whether confessing or staying together, the other person has a trustworthy nature. 'Matters are slow' implies that the relationship progresses slowly, perhaps due to one partner being slow to warm up or external factors, but 'no mistake' ensures a harmonious outcome. Gu transforming into Ding: Gu is like hidden gaps to be mended, Ding like a new feast to be savored. Singles should first heal old wounds, remove emotional 'Gu', then welcome a new person. For couples with accumulated grievances, honest resolution is needed, then feelings will grow deeper. Xun governs entering; gentle words and slow persuasion are more effective than force. In interaction: base on trust, share heartfelt matters; avoid repeated suspicion or bringing up the past. Conducive to subtle communication like letters, shared meals (Ding cooking image); slow-burning love often lasts longest.
health: 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' in health indicates that the physical foundation can be trusted; no major problems for now. 'Matters are slow' means that chronic illness or recovery takes time; impatience is useless. 'No mistake' indicates a good prognosis. Gu hexagram: upper Gen is mountain, belonging to Earth, governs spleen and stomach; transformed Ding hexagram: lower Xun is wind, belonging to Wood, governs liver and gallbladder. Gu to Ding: Earth deficiency, Wood overacting; guard against spleen and stomach stagnation due to improper diet, or liver and gallbladder dysfunction. Health direction: eat light, well-cooked food (Ding cooking nature); avoid raw, cold, greasy; daily routine: rise and rest slowly, especially take a short rest after meals. 'Slow' means morning exercise not too early; wait for daylight. Watch for signs: recurrent bloating, belching, bitter morning mouth — signs of unresolved 'Gu'; treat gently, avoid harsh remedies.
study: 'It can be entrusted; it can be committed' in studies indicates that talent can be trusted, teachers can place hope; it is not a lack of intelligence, only 'matters are slow.' Progress may be delayed, grades not outstanding, often due to unresolved 'Gu' in knowledge — weak foundations obstruct new learning. Gu to Ding matches the need to identify and fill gaps: first rectify old study habits, then establish new methods, like Ding removing the old and adopting the new. Xun governs entering, suitable for careful deliberation and repeated memorization, not for cramming. Exam luck: performance will have 'no mistake,' but prepare by visiting the exam location early and bringing all tools to avoid external interference. Specific methods: keep a mistake notebook, clear each 'Gu' one by one; avoid jumping between problems or aimlessly doing many questions. Favor liberal arts and subjects requiring long-term accumulation; Ding image of slow cooking, success comes naturally.
general: The essence of this sign lies in 'entrustment': life matters can be entrusted and trusted; that removes half of anxiety. 'Matters are slow' is not bad luck but a time for tempering, like Gu transforming into Ding: only after rectification can renewal occur. Three action suggestions: 1) Choose trustworthy people or platforms to delegate affairs; do not bear all burdens alone. 2) List all decaying elements, first clear old problems, then plan new structures. 3) Hold the faith of 'no mistake'; whenever encountering delays, recite this phrase to steady the mind. In mindset, cultivate the virtue of Xun compliance: gentle yet unbreakable, find precision in slowness. Avoid impatient success or constant changes; remember that the taste of a Ding feast is most fragrant with a slow, steady flame. Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment.

sign_text:
Fear and anxious worry,
All before your eyes.
If you meet a clear mirror,
Truth will dawn and falsehood dies.
interpretation1: This sign opens with 'fear and anxious worry' pointing to a state of stress and impending pressure. 'Before your eyes' suggests both urgency and obscured vision. The turning point comes with a 'clear mirror'—wise guidance or sudden insight that dispels illusions. The hexagram moves from Qian (Modesty) to Ming Yi (Darkening of the Light), indicating that excessive humility has led to confusion. The key lies in awakening from self-delusion.
career: Your career is in a phase of 'fear and anxious worry,' with workplace pressures and rumors crowding in. The hexagram advises restraint rather than confrontation. Seek guidance from a senior or neutral party who can see through the issues. The right moment comes after such clarity. Support roles (planning, coordination) are favorable; avoid front-line confrontation. In competitions, let opponents tire themselves and focus on clarifying facts.
wealth: Financial anxiety clouds your judgment. The 'clear mirror' represents sober financial thinking or professional advice. The hexagram warns against both excessive caution and rash moves. Review accounts and contracts carefully; ignore hearsay. Steady income is stable but speculative ventures hide pitfalls. Act slowly; test small amounts. Favorable sectors: earth-related industries or stable savings.
love: Fear and suspicion loom over relationships. Small issues may seem ominous. 'Clear mirror' symbolizes honest communication or an outsider's perspective. Singles: past fears may be self-created; clarify your own heart first. Couples: accumulated misunderstandings cause tension. Open up with vulnerability; avoid dwelling on past errors. A sincere talk can dissolve illusions and restore trust.
health: The sign points to emotional strain—worry harming the lungs (Dui palace) and fear affecting kidneys. Symptoms: shortness of breath, insomnia, vague discomfort. The 'clear mirror' calls for proper medical insight or self-awareness. Focus on calming the mind: morning walks, calligraphy, meditation. Avoid late-night brooding or self-diagnosis. Warning signs: chest tightness, unexplained tension—address them early.
study: Academic anxiety blocks clarity. The 'clear mirror' could be a teacher's advice or a sudden insight. The hexagram advises humility and seeking help; avoid isolated grinding. Discussion groups and consulting seniors are effective. Exam luck improves after gaining perspective. Avoid guessing or cramming; understand the structure. Liberal arts instincts are sharp now.
general: The essence: 'Worry is empty; illumination brings passage.' Two actions: 1) Quiet self-reflection daily—write down worries to see their hollowness. 2) Seek a trusted advisor, unashamed. Remember the Ming Yi principle: 'use darkness to achieve clarity.' Accept temporary confusion as the prelude to insight.

sign_text:
The moon is veiled amid clouds,
The path is lost in haze.
When clouds part and the moon shines clear,
Step forward in gentle ways.
interpretation1: The image of the moon hidden by clouds signifies a period where direction is obscured. The hexagram moves from Jing (the Well) to Xu (Waiting), advising cultivation of inner virtue while awaiting the right moment. 'Gradual progress' is the core: take small steps once the light appears. Though unfavorable in fortune, it promises eventual clarity if patience is kept.
career: Obstructions in your career—superiors who do not understand the situation or hidden obstacles. The hexagrams advise staying grounded and not forcing change. Progress will come after misunderstandings clear. Avoid travel or major decisions now. When the 'moon shines,' consider job changes or bids. Beware of acting on impatience or confronting authority.
wealth: Financial prospects are clouded. Steady income holds, but speculative gains are dim. The hexagrams encourage saving and waiting. After clouds part, salary adjustments or stable returns may come. Avoid high-risk investments. Favorable industries: water, wood, logistics, culture—but only after timing improves. Review expenses and avoid guarantees for others.
love: Emotionally, the moon hidden by clouds suggests unspoken feelings or growing distance. Singles: early impressions may not last; give time for true nature to emerge. Couples: avoid arguments for now; compromise rather than force clarity. When the moon clears, heartfelt communication can deepen bonds. Avoid dwelling on past grievances.
health: The moon relates to kidney yin; clouds indicate sluggish metabolism and mental fatigue. Overwork or anxiety causes false heat (insomnia, dry eyes, body aches). Recovery is gradual: gentle morning walks, simple diet, and calming the mind. Watch for dizziness and cold limbs. Do not rush into vigorous exercise.
study: Study obstacles: difficulty grasping concepts, especially in logic and math. The hexagrams advise reviewing basics before deeper work. Breakthroughs may come near exams. Morning reading, afternoon practice, evening error review. Avoid late-night cramming or comparing with others. Gradual steps yield results.
general: This sign is not an end but a phase. When the moon is hidden, guard your heart and position. When it clears, move slowly and humbly. The key is 'gradual.' Three things: 1) Daily quiet reflection; 2) Consult a trusted person before decisions; 3) Live simply to conserve energy. The moon is never truly lost; patience will reveal its light.

sign_text:
Loud calls along the road,
Yet not a ripple of strife.
Times are hard, the mind in disorder;
All depends on a helper in life.
interpretation1: The first line depicts external clamor and temptations. The second line hints at hidden dangers beneath a calm surface. The core issue is inner turmoil due to adverse timing. The solution lies in a 'helper,' not necessarily a high status person but a clear-sighted advisor. The hexagrams move from Jian (Obstruction) to Qian (Modesty), advising humility and seeking support.
career: Dominating meetings or bypassing your leader is unwise.
wealth: Financial information is overwhelming. Apparent calm hides risk. Emotion-driven decisions lead to impulsive spending or following hype. Rely on a steady advisor or partner. Steady income is better than speculation. Favorable: savings, fixed assets, agriculture. Avoid high leverage or guarantees. Small losses may occur in late autumn; budget ahead.
love: External distractions in love: many suitors but unclear intentions; friends' opinions; social pressures. Surface harmony but underlying communication blockages. Inner turmoil magnifies flaws. A mediator—family, mutual friend, or partner's initiative—can help. For singles, formal introductions are better than social media. Couples: schedule unhurried talks; avoid silent treatment.
health: Sudden environmental changes (season shift, work pace) may bring mild illness. Kidney and throat issues may go unnoticed. Emotional imbalance (insomnia, anxiety) is the root. Seek a good doctor or natural surroundings. Guard against digestive issues and shoulder/back pain. Daily kidney-strengthening exercises, afternoon naps, root vegetables. Warning: persistent palpitations, dizziness, appetite loss—seek professional diagnosis.
study: Study distractions: activities, competition, information overload. Grades appear stable but hidden gaps exist. Inner disorder leads to careless errors. Seek a teacher’s guidance; be humble. Exam luck requires repeated practice of old problems. Early arrival at exam venues is beneficial. Focus on foundational subjects; avoid shortcuts. Study groups and senior advice help; do not isolate or guess questions.
general: The outer calm hides inner turmoil. The hexagram change shows the key is humility: hide your sharpness, and a helper will come. When facing loud choices, pause a day before deciding. Admit your confusion and seek others’ counsel. When times are hard, retreat to cultivate the mind. Remember: a helper is attracted by your humility, not your pride.

sign_text:
By the deep pool you cast your line,
In purity without a speck.
The giant turtle follows your will;
No need to doubt or check.
interpretation1: Casting a line at the abyss symbolizes taking action at a critical juncture. 'Purity without a speck' describes a clear mind and undisturbed environment. The giant turtle represents a great reward that comes naturally when the time is ripe. The hexagrams move from Fu (Return) to Zhen (Thunder), indicating that a fresh start and vigorous energy lead to success. The fortune is very favorable, not by luck, but by alignment of heart and action.
career: Your career is at a deep pool—challenging projects hold great opportunities. Focus single-mindedly, ignore distractions, and take on core tasks. Success in technical breakthroughs, bids, or field expansion. The time is right for young and middle-aged leaders to shine. Do not compete for credit or dabble in multiple things. Trust your judgment after thorough preparation.
wealth: Wealth comes from deep specialization, not shallow ventures. Choose one area of expertise and stay focused. A major inflow is likely—returns from long-term investment, patents, or unexpected gain. Avoid speculation. Favorable sectors: earth-related (real estate, agriculture) or traditional culture. Do not panic at minor fluctuations; trust your chosen path.
love: Love requires deep, sincere effort. Stay pure in intention—no hidden agendas. Singles: active approach in a calm setting (book club, tea house) may attract a true partner. Existing couples: relationship is solid; marriage may come naturally. If issues arise, communicate openly without testing. Spring (Fu and Zhen energy) is ideal for declarations.
health: Health lies in purity and calm. Strengthen spleen (Kun) and liver/gallbladder (Zhen). In spring, avoid liver yang rising. Keep diet simple, drink water, avoid heavy food. Gentle morning walks and seasonal greens are beneficial. Avoid late-night thoughts, spicy food, alcohol. Warning signs: dry mouth, bitter morning taste—early to bed, early to rise.
study: Academic depth requires casting your line in deep waters—tackle difficult subjects head-on. Focus without distractions; results will follow. Excellent for exams or thesis defense; trust your intuition. Logic and innovation subjects benefit. Build a solid foundation. Exam luck is high; review structure rather than memorize. Stable study groups help without interference.
general: Opportunity hides in depth. Keep a clear mind and decisive hand. Three actions: 1) Face major choices bravely; 2) Create a pure, quiet environment; 3) Accept successes without self-doubt. 'Purity without a speck' is the foundation—respond to events without clinging. The fortune lies in the balance of stillness and courage.

interpretation1: 'Clouds part, the moon returns to full' signals that the veil will clear, the moon reappears—adornment stripped away, truth shines.
career: The line 'From nothingness, uprightness shall arise' suggests hidden opportunities in seemingly hopeless positions or projects. Bi hexagram indicates your current work may involve surface-level tasks, but the transformation to Li calls for displaying your true abilities. 'Your cares may turn to sorrow' warns of temporary setbacks such as performance reviews or competition. 'Clouds part, the moon returns to full' predicts resolution around the full moon (around the 15th of the lunar month). 'A thousand miles, the urgent sail boat flies' urges decisive action once the turning point appears. With Gen (stillness) transforming to Li (movement), it's time to advance rather than maintain the status quo. Advice: Actively volunteer for key tasks; break free from routine. Favorable for job interviews, presentations, and cross-department collaboration. Consider moving south or pursuing fire-element industries (culture, internet). Avoid procrastination, over-polishing your resume, or competing with superiors for credit.
wealth: 'From nothingness, uprightness shall arise' indicates financial opportunities that appear elusive but are real if pursued through righteous means. The Bi hexagram warns against illusory wealth (speculation, vanity spending); as it transforms to Li, discernment returns. 'Your cares may turn to sorrow' cautions against greed; if anxious about money, remember 'Clouds part, the moon returns to full'—financial cycles ebb and flow. 'A thousand miles, the urgent sail boat flies' suggests swift income, perhaps an unexpected windfall, but requires steady steering. The hexagram shows metal refined by fire: main income stable, incidental gains fleeting. Wealth trend: first blocked, then smooth. Advice: Regular salary may increase; pursue performance bonuses. For investments, favor long-term holdings; avoid speculative stocks. Cooperation benefits fire-related industries (electricity, catering, culture). Avoid borrowing for expansion, insider tips, or high-risk ventures.
love: This sign indicates initial worry followed by joy. 'Your cares may turn to sorrow' reflects singles' frustration in seeking a partner or existing partners' secret doubts. The Bi hexagram suggests initial pretense in relationships. However, 'Clouds part, the moon returns to full' is very favorable—misunderstandings will clear, or fate will arrive unexpectedly; the full moon symbolizes completeness. Gen (stillness) and Li (clarity) call for honest communication, shedding pretense. 'A thousand miles, the urgent sail boat flies' hints that romance may progress rapidly once it starts. For singles: seize social opportunities, especially around the full moon; don't be held back by past sorrows. For couples: past conflicts can be resolved through this sign; take the initiative to reconcile. Avoid hiding feelings, dwelling on old grudges, or hesitating and missing good opportunities.
health: Exercise: gentle tai chi or Ba Duan Jin (Eight Brocades); avoid heavy sweating.
study: 'From nothingness, uprightness shall arise' in studies suggests finding true knowledge where none seems apparent. The Bi hexagram warns against superficial memorization; transforming to Li requires understanding and seeking truth. 'Your cares may turn to sorrow' indicates pre-exam anxiety or weakness in certain subjects, but 'Clouds part, the moon returns to full' promises sudden clarity. 'A thousand miles, the urgent sail boat flies' implies the exam period is pressing—quick review and speed techniques are beneficial. The original hexagram Gen (stillness) in Bi suggests stopping at the surface; transformation to Li (fire) demands insight. Exam luck: perform well under pressure, but watch for carelessness. Favorable for liberal arts (divergent thinking) and sudden problem-solving in sciences; full moon days are especially auspicious. Avoid procrastination, mindless drilling without reflection, or narrowing your focus too much.
general: Overall, this sign moves from initial chaos and sorrow to a breakthrough of clarity and swift action—a very favorable sign. The four lines encourage: find the straight path where there seems none; let sorrow refine you; persist until the clouds part; and when the wind fills your sails, move quickly. Key actions: (1) Uphold integrity without pretense; meet the world authentically. (2) When the turning point arrives, act without delay. (3) Remember that sorrow is temporary; broaden your heart and circumstances will shift. Gen palace's Bi transforming to Li teaches that clinging to adornment leads to darkness, while acting with clarity opens the way. Shed the superficial, embrace substance, and use Li's light to illuminate your path. Those who receive this sign should hold the moon in their hearts and raise their sails swiftly—the world will open wide.

interpretation1: The poem's first line emphasizes that creation follows nature's inherent laws; do not force what cannot be forced. The Kun hexagram represents receptivity and nurturing, but the changing line into Shi introduces hidden danger within smooth circumstances. 'A thousand-mile campaign brings blessings line by line' refers to the Shi (army) hexagram, which implies toil, travel, and mobilizing many—the 'blessings' are not immediate but come after hardship. 'Success in fame with divine aid is shown' indicates that achievements are not solely due to human effort; they require heavenly timing and support, and only those who follow proper discipline receive such aid. 'When Snake meets Rabbit, change will then be known' points to the cyclical encounter of earthly branches Si (Snake) and Mao (Rabbit), signaling a major shift in fortune. The sign as a whole presents a path from compliance to conflict, from stillness to action—blessings may be abundant, but only after a thousand-mile journey. Impatience or reckless action invites disaster; it is advisable to remain steadfast, await the right moment, and rely on divine help to navigate the transition.
career: The phrase 'a thousand-mile campaign' directly evokes a battlefield in the workplace—intense competition, travel, or departmental restructuring. Kun transforming into Shi means moving from passive compliance to active engagement, but with caution. 'Success in fame with divine aid' hints that promotions are not purely merit-based; they may require patron support or unexpected opportunities. The key is to display your talents publicly and leverage team efforts. 'When Snake meets Rabbit' signals changes in personnel or organizational structure; pay attention to Si and Mao years (e.g., 2025, 2027) and plan accordingly. This sign advises against independent, aggressive moves or clashing with superiors. Instead, take on coordinating roles, using gentleness to overcome rigidity. If considering a job change, wait until the transition becomes clear; otherwise, you may be caught in a whirlpool.
wealth: Wealth is symbolized by 'a thousand-mile campaign'—money comes like army provisions, earned through hard work and long journeys, not from speculation. Kun hexagram relates to land and storage, but transforming into Shi warns of losses due to competition. Therefore, financial management should focus on stable savings and avoid high-risk expansion. 'Blessings line by line' points to long-term returns, such as pensions or long-term project dividends, not quick profits. The Snake-Rabbit encounter indicates volatility in wealth; during Si and Mao years, reduce leverage and be wary of partnership disputes or guarantee issues. Specific advice: main income industries are agriculture, real estate, logistics; for sideline income, collectibles might be considered but avoid borrowed investments. The phrase 'divine aid' reminds that if unexpected money comes, it's wise to donate a portion to cultivate good fortune, otherwise it may leave as quickly as it came.
love: 'Creation's course is nature's own design'—love should follow fate; forcing it harms. Kun transforming into Shi suggests the relationship moves from harmony to conflict, or emotional distance due to separation ('a thousand-mile campaign'). 'Blessings line by line' implies deep affection, but it must undergo trials. For married or partnered individuals, current pressures and external temptations require trust and commitment. Singles: 'Success in fame with divine aid' indicates that a good match may come through friends or unexpected encounters; don't rush. 'When Snake meets Rabbit' directly points to emotional upheaval—in Snake or Rabbit years, major changes like breakup or marriage may occur; choose carefully. Advice: actively communicate to resolve conflicts. Avoid: bringing up old grudges, acting on impulse. Long-distance relationships may benefit from the campaign imagery: brief separations can actually deepen bonds. If long-standing arguments persist, change is imminent; cultivate yourself and wait for the right moment.
health: Kun hexagram governs the spleen, stomach, and muscles; transforming into Shi can cause stagnation of earth energy and blockage of water, leading to 'a thousand-mile campaign' representing fatigue. Watch for digestive issues, limb soreness, and lowered immunity. The Snake-Rabbit encounter marks a change in energy flow; chronic conditions may surface or worsen. 'Creation's course is nature's own design' advises against worrying over temporary test results—health follows natural rhythms; avoid overmedication. Gentle exercise like tai chi or walking is beneficial; diet should be bland and spleen-nourishing, avoiding raw or cold foods. Shi hexagram includes the danger of water, so also pay attention to kidney energy and bone/joint health; those traveling frequently should guard against accidental injury. 'Divine aid' may come through a good doctor; if illness occurs, seek medical help actively and follow rest orders to receive lasting blessings.
study: Study is likened to 'a thousand-mile campaign'—it requires discipline and endurance. 'Success in fame with divine aid' emphasizes that exam luck depends not only on ability but also on mental state and question fit; before exams, calm your mind and seek guidance from teachers. Kun transforming into Shi: solid foundation but the Shi hexagram indicates danger—exams may feature tricky or unconventional questions. 'When Snake meets Rabbit' signals major turning points in admissions or school choices during Si or Mao years, such as changing majors or transferring schools. Advice: set long-term plans, refine methods; avoid guessing exam questions or sporadic effort. This sign favors accumulative disciplines like literature, history, and philosophy; for sciences, double your practice to compensate for weaker foundations.
general: This sign summarizes 'hardship first, blessings later; danger within opportunity.' Creation is natural; do not blame fortune. The thousand-mile campaign is exactly the training ground for your character. Blessings do not come from idle sitting; divine aid awaits your own cultivation. Key actions: (1) In all matters, prefer defense over offense; postpone major decisions until the Snake-Rabbit transition is clear. (2) Cultivate team awareness and coordination; avoid lone-wolf tactics. (3) When encountering bottlenecks, return to the virtue of Kun—gentle, receptive, and grounded—to bear the danger of Shi and transform misfortune into good. Mentally, trust the natural flow; don't be discouraged by temporary setbacks, nor arrogant over small successes. An unfavorable sign is not a dead end but a navigational light, illuminating the thousand-mile road ahead—every step brings you closer to lasting fortune.

interpretation1: The first line speaks of relying on strong righteousness or external force to sweep away obstacles, bringing temporary clarity. However, the essence of this unfavorable sign lies in the subsequent lines: even after demons are eliminated, the power must not be withdrawn. The Ding hexagram represents establishment and renewal; if slackness follows, it collapses into Wei Ji, a state of disarray. Li Palace's Ding transforming into Wei Ji means fire and water do not interact; creation is followed by failure. 'Exert the might with all your strength' warns that guarding success is harder than achieving it. 'Remember well, lest you fall at length' explicitly states that forgetting past hardships will lead to a second downfall. The image is like a warrior who, after defeating demons, relaxes and is himself devoured. Core message: no victory is permanent; the greatest danger after success is complacency. Only by maintaining vigilance as at the beginning can long-term stability be secured.
career: Avoid: neglecting personnel after celebrations, being unprepared for counterattack. Advice: regularly review your strategy; maintain the same caution and diligence as when you started.
wealth: The line 'all demons disappear' but later risk of falling suggests that wealth may be gained through breaking through difficulties, but the path is unstable and easily lost. The Ding hexagram indicates that main income is acceptable; transforming into Wei Ji warns that side income is deceptive and investments easily become chaotic. 'Exert the might with all your strength' advises extreme caution after gaining money. Wealth trend is like panning for gold in the waves: protect your principal. Main income from regular job is the base; fire-related industries (culture, energy) are favorable. 'Remember well' means learn from past losses and avoid repeating mistakes. Advice: recover funds early, reduce borrowing. Avoid: following speculative trends, trusting 'sure-win' projects, and ambiguous partnerships that can lead to financial disputes.
love: The 'demons' in the sign symbolize relationship obstacles or external interference; after 'relying on mighty power', temporary peace may come. But 'after vanquishing, maintain the power near' indicates that maintaining a relationship is like sailing against the current—rest and you fall behind. Ding transforming into Wei Ji suggests a shift from stability to estrangement; fire and water not mixing may cause communication breakdown. For singles, intense pursuit may succeed, but if passion cools quickly, the relationship may wither. For those in partnerships, do not neglect inner feelings once external pressures are removed; 'exert the might' means listen and respond with full attention, remembering the original intention to avoid a long-term fall. Approach: regularly reflect on the relationship's state together. Avoid: bringing up old grievances, stonewalling. In times of crisis, treat the relationship as seriously as fighting demons.
health: After 'all demons disappear', one must still 'exert might'. Ding transforming into Wei Ji, Li palace governs fire; Ding is a vessel, Wei Ji indicates disharmony of fire and water. This can correspond to recurring cardiovascular issues, inflammation, or heat syndromes—especially relapse after temporary recovery due to negligence. Li fire rising can damage yin; Ding to Wei Ji may manifest as digestive imbalances, upper heat and lower cold. 'Remember well' stresses that post-illness care must be continuous; 'mighty power' must be sustained to maintain healthy energy. Health direction: regular routine to nourish yin and balance yang; light diet avoiding spicy and greasy foods; gentle exercise like tai chi, avoiding sudden stops or starts. Watch for: palpitations when tired, recurrent mouth inflammations, unstable sleep—do not ignore minor symptoms.
study: 'Rely on mighty power' is like a student achieving results through cramming or talent, but 'after vanquishing, maintain the power near' directly points to the need for sustained effort. Ding to Wei Ji: if the foundation is unstable, studies collapse. Li fire's brightness requires continuous fuel. This sign indicates that intelligence can reach certain heights but perseverance is lacking; exam luck is good at first but dangerous later; especially avoid relaxing before completion. Make long-term plans; even for strengths, 'exert might' by daily practice. 'Remember well' means summarize past experiences, keep error logs to prevent repeating mistakes. Favorable for logical reasoning subjects, but Li fire is changeable; avoid being half-hearted. Exam advice: complete preparation early; avoid last-minute cramming. After one exam, don't indulge and affect the next.
general: This unfavorable sign is more a warning than a disaster. Without careful management, there is indeed a risk of 'falling at length'. After victory, one must be as vigilant as when facing demons; mighty power is not one-time but requires continuous exertion. The hexagram Ding transforming to Wei Ji clearly shows that the shift from order to chaos can happen in a moment. Key actions: (1) 'Remember'—deeply internalize the reasons for success and the lessons from failure. (2) 'Exert might'—maintain daily alertness and diligence; do not let achievements dissipate. (3) 'Sustain power'—maintain boundaries and leave room; do not show all your edges. Mentally, approach every smooth stretch as if walking on thin ice; this will turn the unfavorable sign into a path of long-term stability.

interpretation1: The first two lines clearly state that the path of cultivation requires unwavering determination; with a firm heart, the Way can be attained. 'Achieve great deeds, then early depart' echoes the principle of 'retire after success' from the Tao Te Ching—build accomplishments but do not cling to them. 'Build a thatched hut, refine your inner art' shifts to a return to simplicity, using a humble hut to cultivate a pure essence. 'One day you soar, immortal's rank you'll own' indicates that when the work is complete, you will ascend to enlightenment. The poem forms a complete sequence: determination, achievement, retreat, cultivation, and transcendence. The hexagram belongs to Xun Palace: Wu Wang (Without Falsehood) transforming into Sui (Following). Wu Wang's judgment 'not favorable to have somewhere to go' warns against rash actions; Sui's Commentary emphasizes 'the meaning of following the times.' The transition from Wu Wang to Sui shows movement from anxious rigidity to flowing with circumstances. Xun is wind, whose virtue is penetration and humility. In summary, this sign advises holding to true thoughts and progressing steadily, but upon achieving fame, do not forget to turn inward for cultivation—only then can you reach perfection.
career: The phrase 'achieve great deeds' indicates opportunities for notable accomplishments, especially in fields that require pioneering concentration. 'Then early depart' reminds you to prepare an exit strategy when success is near; do not cling to authority. Wu Wang hexagram advises against improper demands, so career progress must follow right paths, avoiding shortcuts. Sui hexagram suggests adapting to circumstances. Xun (wind) governs communication and coordination; suitable industries include culture, education, and consulting. Specific advice: early stage should be 'steadfast heart'—focus on core tasks; after promotion or project completion, shift to mentoring or behind-the-scenes coordination. Avoid: claiming credit, refusing to let go after success. Advice: timely summarize and hand over frontline work, saving energy for the next phase.
wealth: The lines 'build a thatched hut, refine your inner art' imply that material wealth need not be abundant; simplicity is sufficient. Wu Wang hexagram's third line ('disaster of no falsehood') suggests wealth comes and goes unpredictably. 'One day you soar' warns that attachment to possessions becomes a burden. Thus, wealth trend is mainly stable; main income is reliable, but side income from investments is not favorable. Sui hexagram advises going with the flow; financial management should be conservative, reducing high-risk allocations. Specific advice: salary income is stable; consider savings or buying value-preserving physical assets. Avoid: rash expansion, stock market speculation. If unexpected money arrives, use it for charity or family necessities. Mentally, know contentment; avoid comparison.
love: no grand vows but a steady flow
health: 'Refine your inner art' directly points to nurturing the body as nurturing the true self; it requires a 'steadfast heart.' Wu Wang hexagram's fifth line ('no falsehood, no medicine, joy') reminds that minor ailments need not be heavily medicated; let the body heal naturally. Xun (wind) corresponds to the liver, gallbladder, and respiratory system, also governs tendons; watch for liver qi stagnation, colds, cramps. Specific health practices: morning meditation, tai chi, ba duan jin—gentle exercises help smooth qi. 'Build a thatched hut' symbolizes a quiet living environment; spend time in nature, avoid noisy, stimulating places. Diet should be light, reduce sour flavors, increase sweet to nourish the spleen. Warning sign: chronic late nights and overwork may deplete true energy; when this happens, immediately return to regular routines.
study: 'The Way demands a steadfast heart; with steadfast heart, the Way is earned' is a maxim for studies: only persistent effort leads to deep understanding. Xun hexagram means penetration, indicating strong comprehension; good for subjects that require contemplation, like literature, history, philosophy, and arts. 'Achieve great deeds' can be extended to exam success, thesis publication, or competition awards. Wu Wang transforming into Sui: Wu Wang warns against careless mistakes—details matter; Sui suggests following teachers' and peers' guidance, not being stubborn. Exam luck: early preparation needs steadfast heart; during the exam, stay calm and don't let one difficult question upset you. Advice: set long-term plans, join study groups. Avoid: last-minute cramming, studying alone in isolation. If stuck, 'then early depart' means returning to basics and reviewing fundamental concepts.
general: The entire sign is built around 'refinement of the true self,' emphasizing unwavering original intention and proper timing of advance and retreat. The core can be summarized as 'engage the world to build achievements, then retreat to cultivate the heart': in all matters, follow the right path and do your best, but at the peak, know when to step back to achieve a good ending. Three concrete actions: (1) After reaching a career or life goal, voluntarily let go of vanity and turn inward for development. (2) Reduce unnecessary desires; live simply to nourish your spirit. (3) Set aside a few minutes daily for quiet reflection, like building a mental 'thatched hut.' Mentally, avoid greed and haste; remember that even 'one day you soar' is built on accumulated efforts. Follow this Way, and even in the mundane world, you will find peace of mind and clarity of purpose.

sign_text:
One thought ascends to heaven, one thought descends to hell;
Heaven and hell at once divide glory and disgrace.
Set your own mind truly firm,
And do not follow the crowd in a dull and weary pace.
interpretation1: The sign verse, 'One thought ascends to heaven, one thought descends to hell,' concisely points to the mind as the pivot of fortune. Heaven symbolizes light and smoothness; hell represents darkness and hardship. A single turn of thought separates the two paths, hence 'divide glory and disgrace.' The latter line, 'Set your own mind truly firm, and do not follow the crowd in a dull and weary pace,' deepens the message: the key to shifting thoughts lies in establishing true intention and perseverance, not drifting with the tide. This hexagram belongs to the Gen Palace, with Zhong Fu (Inner Trust) transforming into Yi (Increase). Zhong Fu has the image of wind over the lake, symbolizing sincerity and correct conduct; Yi has wind over thunder, indicating dynamic action yielding benefit. Gen is the mountain, whose virtue is stillness—aligned with the sign's message of 'stopping thought in truth.' Overall, the sign reveals that fortune and misfortune arise from the mind, not from external circumstances. If you hold to true thoughts and maintain sincerity, even adversity can turn to safety; if you give rise to false thoughts and follow others mindlessly, even flat ground may stir up waves.
career: On the career path, gains and losses often hang on a single thought. 'One thought ascends to heaven, one thought descends to hell' warns you to be extremely cautious at critical decisions. Zhong Fu transforming into Yi indicates that sincerity leads to substantive progress. Therefore, career favors independent responsibility and adherence to principle; avoid echoing others. Specifically, if you face a job change, transfer, or project dispute, reflect: does your intention come from true inner conviction? Matters where you 'set your own mind truly,' such as independently proposing a new research initiative or upholding quality standards against market hype, often lead to a bright turn. When acting decisively, the Yi hexagram favors moving forward; but if you follow the crowd, you may lose the trust of Zhong Fu and invite suspicion. Avoid: adjusting standards due to personal relationships in a group, or abandoning your own views out of eagerness to fit in.
wealth: Financial ups and downs arise from thoughts. The metaphor of heaven and hell mirrors the consequences of greed versus integrity: greed, even if it brings temporary profit, eventually leads to delusion; integrity, even with slow progress, reaches higher ground. Zhong Fu represents trust, Yi represents profit—the hexagrams indicate that wealth comes from honest accumulation, not speculation. Thus, the financial outlook favors steady main income; be cautious with windfall gains. Sources of income should focus on your primary field, relying on professional service or quality reputation. 'Set your own mind truly' applies here. If you chase trends or blindly partner, you may end up like the crowd—adding to anxiety. In financial management, adhere to discipline, such as regular savings or investing in your area of expertise. Avoid: being swayed by others into unknown projects, especially those promising 'quick doubling.' The Gen Palace advises knowing when to stop—do not covet undue gains.
love: Relationships also begin with a single thought. 'One thought ascends to heaven, one thought descends to hell' illustrates how kindness and resentment can shift in an instant: resentment turns a good match into bitter enemies; forgiveness transforms hell into a lotus pool. Zhong Fu emphasizes sincerity, Yi emphasizes benefit—mutual affection requires true hearts. 'Set your own mind truly' is the key. For singles: do not follow the crowd—do not lower your standards or rush into a relationship due to others' opinions. Hold to what you truly seek, and wait for the right connection. For those in ambiguous relationships: be straightforward; once 'true' is established, clarity follows. For couples: fortune depends on daily thoughts—when conflict arises, reflect on your own mind rather than comparing with others. The trust of Zhong Fu can turn discord into harmony. Avoid: bringing up past grievances or letting external comparisons affect your relationship.
health: Physical and mental well-being are tied to the flux of thoughts. The heaven-hell imagery aligns with the impact of extreme emotions on the organs: excessive joy may affect the heart, anger the liver, and fear the kidneys. The Gen Palace represents stillness, Zhong Fu inner joy, Yi wind and thunder—nurturing health lies in calming false thoughts and soothing the spirit. 'Set your own mind truly' means cultivating mindfulness, undisturbed by external noise. Health direction favors quiet cultivation: morning meditation, evening reflection, allowing scattered thoughts to settle like wind returning to the lake. Watch for insomnia, dizziness, or palpitations related to overthinking. Maintain a light diet, reduce spicy foods. Avoid comparing your health with others to minimize anxiety. Trust your own rhythm to attain the peace of the mountain.
study: In learning, the key is to establish true intention and avoid blind following. 'Set your own mind truly, and do not follow the crowd' directly addresses students' pitfalls: plagiarism, trend-chasing, and parroting. Zhong Fu transforming into Yi means sincerity in learning brings advancement. Examinations favor genuine knowledge, not shortcuts. Academic fortune favors independent thinking and deep specialization—choose your own topics, reject superficial trends. Humanities students should avoid echoing popular opinions; science students should not apply formulas without understanding. For exams, those who study with true intention perform well; those who follow the crowd will face a stark contrast. Create a personalized study plan; avoid comparing progress with others. The trust of Zhong Fu will eventually yield the abundance of Yi.
general: Thus, the turning point lies entirely in a single thought. Reflect on how this resonates with your current situation.

interpretation1: The sign opens with 'a sheep leaves the flock'—sheep are gentle and gregarious, so this departure signals a crack in an otherwise stable situation. 'Facing the people day by day': the 'day' symbolizes the sovereign or light, shining upon all, indicating matters have come to light and cannot be avoided—public opinion or open judgment is needed. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites' is a sudden turn; the ox's mouth represents a trap or constraint—encountering it brings harm, hinting that beneath a peaceful surface lie hidden conflicts, with disputes or interest clashes ready to erupt. 'One lord, one minister—lord and minister are startled' points to the core contradiction in hierarchical relationships: power and responsibility are out of balance, causing mutual suspicion. The final line, 'they must rely on the true spirit to save the multitudes,' suggests that human effort alone is insufficient—higher wisdom or public justice must be invoked to resolve the conflict. The hexagram belongs to the Qian Palace, with Pi (Stagnation) transforming into Song (Conflict). Pi represents heaven and earth not communicating, blockage; Song arises from verbal disputes. The progression from Pi to Song shows long-accumulated blockage finally erupting into arguments. Qian Palace is metal, strong and sharp, fueling a competitive mindset. The sign indicates that while the situation is not yet extremely unlucky, hidden troubles have emerged. Avoid stubborn aggression; rely on the 'true spirit'—justice, professional mediation, or a person of high moral standing—to turn danger into safety.
career: Your career fortune is like 'a sheep leaves the flock'—team cohesion weakens, or a member becomes detached. Watch for slack due to unclear responsibilities. 'Facing the people day by day' points to public evaluation or the attention of superiors; your actions will be scrutinized. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites' warns of verbal traps or interest conflicts—once you step into another's territory (the ox's mouth), you may be attacked. Qian Palace favors initiative, but the transformation from Pi to Song warns: do not rely on strength to contend, or you may fall into prolonged struggle. Specifically, this sign favors independent projects or open bidding. Avoid direct confrontation in meetings. Roles suited to technical positions or impartial coordination; avoid getting involved in factional politics. 'Lord and minister startled' suggests possible management changes—stay in your role, speak little and act much. Seek the 'true spirit'—procedural justice or endorsement from a senior figure—to calm the turmoil.
wealth: Finances are represented by 'a sheep leaves the flock'—scattered sheep mean dispersed income, difficulty accumulating. Regular income may be stable but extra expenses rise. 'Facing the people day by day' indicates finances must be conducted in the open; hidden operations risk disaster. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites' directly warns of investment traps, contract loopholes—once you touch them, loss follows. Pi hexagram indicates blockage in income; transforming into Song raises the risk of disputes over money, such as account conflicts or partnership breakdowns. The Qian Palace is strong metal—advises defense over offense. Financial management should be stable and cautious; avoid high-risk speculation, private lending, or oral promises. Favor real-asset industries (metal, earth) for main income; defer side ventures like stocks. Key times are the year or month of the Ox—extra care with details. Seek the 'true spirit'—consult professionals to review contracts and avoid verbal disputes.
love: Romance: 'a sheep leaves the flock' suggests one partner becomes distant, losing former warmth. Singles may encounter someone indecisive. 'Facing the people day by day' reveals that relationship problems can no longer be hidden—honest dialogue is needed. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites' points to quarrels over small issues, like stepping on each other's sensitive spots and hurting deeply. 'Lord and minister startled' mirrors power imbalance in the relationship—one partner's control causes the other unease. Qian Palace is direct and hard, prone to head-on collisions; Pi to Song warns of the harm of cold wars or bringing up past grievances. Advice: couples should actively yield; rely on the 'true spirit'—shared beliefs, elder mediation, or professional counseling. Avoid: revisiting old wounds or attacking weak points. Singles should not force a relationship now; first sort out your own feelings to avoid entering a dangerous situation (ox's mouth). When your state is peaceful, good affinity will appear.
health: 'A sheep leaves the flock' represents scattered energy and irregular routines—a state of easy fatigue. 'Facing the people day by day' brings fire (day) and metal (Qian Palace), fire over metal—potential lung dryness, sore throat, skin sensitivity. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites': the ox's mouth corresponds to the spleen and stomach—dietary indiscipline may cause mouth ulcers, indigestion, or acute inflammation. 'Lord and minister startled' indicates mental anxiety; Pi hexagram blocks Qi, transforming into Song stirs liver fire—easily leading to insomnia, headaches. Health direction: harmonize spleen and stomach; reduce spicy and raw-cold foods to avoid 'ox's mouth' injury; also moisten lungs and clear dryness. Morning walks to soothe the liver; avoid overthinking late at night. Seek the 'true spirit' of calm—through meditation or Tai Chi—to gather the scattered mind. In Ox months or days, be extra vigilant against recurrence of old ailments; do not push through.
study: Studies encounter the 'sheep leaves the flock' image—attention is easily diverted, like sheep scattering. 'Facing the people day by day' brings exams, interviews, or public defense, exposing your performance. 'When it meets an ox's mouth, it bites' refers to unexpected difficult questions or traps, damaging confidence. 'Lord and minister startled' reflects pre-exam anxiety or pressure from teachers/parents. Pi to Song—from blockage to debate—academic breakthrough lies in resolving doubts through discussion. Qian Palace favors logical subjects like math and debate, but avoid obstinacy. Practical methods: study with peers, seek help from good teachers (the 'true spirit') to clear ox-mouth puzzles. Avoid: closed-door cramming or neglecting basics. Before exams, familiarize yourself with the environment; during the exam, be cautious of trap questions. A calm mind will lead to success.
general: This sign uses the metaphors of a stray sheep and an ox's mouth to indicate hidden dangers beneath apparent stability. The fortunate aspect is that the storm has not yet fully struck; the unfortunate aspect is that misjudgment may invite trouble. Overall advice: above all, avoid aggressiveness. Whether in career, money, or emotions, when you encounter the 'ox's mouth,' retreat—do not confront head-on. 'Rely on the true spirit' is the key: when human effort is insufficient, seek external help—procedural justice, a respected person, or quiet introspection. Three key actions: first, for any contract or promise, get everything in writing and handle it formally; second, when conflicts arise, step back to cool down, avoid immediate retaliation; third, actively seek impartial third-party mediation. In mindset, take the 'stray sheep' as a warning: return to the collective and keep to your role to avoid trouble. Hold to the 'true spirit'—reverence and the middle way—then alarm will dissipate, and the multitudes may be saved. Consider this an invitation to self-reflection, not a prediction.

sign_text:
If one is destined to connect,
A single gesture makes them turn back;
But those stubbornly lost,
Even repeated guidance will not make them leave.
interpretation1: The sign verse 'If one is destined to connect, a single finger makes them turn back' suggests that a person with insight can be awakened with the slightest hint; 'a single finger' is like a lamp, symbolizing the subtlety of timing and the rarity of opportunity. 'But those stubbornly lost, even repeated guidance will not make them leave' points directly to a rigid, self-enclosed state—like a cow stuck in mud, external force cannot pull it free. This sign juxtaposes 'delusion' and 'awakening,' warning the seeker not to repeat past mistakes. The hexagram belongs to the Qian Palace, originally strong, but Dun (Retreat) indicates withdrawal, and the change to Pi (Stagnation) shows yin and yang separated, heaven and earth not communicating—like clouds obscuring the sun. The wise act upon the signs, while the foolish remain trapped. Though Qian is strong, now it is in a closed state; if you blindly advance, you will hit a wall. Only sudden awakening and turning back bring vitality. Overall, this sign is not a fixed calamity but a test of the mind—delusion and awakening lie in a single thought. Those who can turn around find a turning point; the obstinate only waste time.
career: Career is like 'those stubbornly lost'—you may be unaware of your own blind spots. 'A single finger makes them turn back' hints that a benefactor or opportunity is near, but you may miss it due to stubbornness. Dun transforming into Pi indicates retreat and blockage—there may be bottlenecks, exclusion, or poor decisions. Avoid competing for power or undertaking unfeasible projects. Timing: changes often occur at the end of spring or autumn; observe the trends. Advised: actively seek advice from seniors or outsiders; consider switching positions, taking a break, or shifting to supportive roles. Avoid: sticking to old methods, arguing with superiors, or expanding against the trend. If your industry shows signs of decline, follow the wisdom of 'turning back' and withdraw early.
wealth: Wealth is like 'repeated guidance will not make them leave'—showing you are lost in desire and unable to extricate yourself. 'If one is destined to connect' echoes that wealth cannot be forced; when timing is not ripe, the more you struggle, the more you sink. Dun (Retreat) and Pi (Stagnation) indicate financial loss, partnership breakdowns, or investment traps. Regular income barely holds; windfalls are mostly traps. Advised: conservative financial management, pay off old debts, avoid high-risk projects temporarily. Consider land or warehousing (Earth-related) fields for temporary stability. Avoid: new borrowing, hype-driven speculation, or joint ventures with relatives. If already trapped, follow 'a single finger turning back'—stop losses in time.
love: On the emotional path, 'stubbornly lost' resembles unrequited love, entanglement, or an inability to let go of a futile attachment. 'If one is destined to connect, a single finger makes them turn back' implies that true affinity has mutual understanding—no force is needed. Dun transforming into Pi represents obstruction and disconnection. Singles should not force a relationship; past obsessions or attachments are clouding your vision—clear your heart and wait. Those in a relationship may suffer from stubbornness leading to cold wars; both need a way out. Advised: singles temporarily leave the circle of attachment, spend time in nature, and a shift in thought may bring new encounters. Couples: take the initiative to show vulnerability; use a small gesture (like a 'single finger') to break the ice. Avoid: repeatedly bringing up past grievances, demanding right or wrong, or controlling the other 'for their own good.'
health: The verse 'stubbornly lost' hints at stagnation of Qi and blood and a closed mind. Qian Palace belongs to metal, governing lungs and large intestine; Dun transforming into Pi indicates blocked Qi flow, leading to excessive worry—possible chest tightness, headaches, or lingering ailments. 'A single finger makes them turn back' implies that a turning point often comes from a simple habit change, such as morning deep breathing or regular meals. Health direction: soothe the liver and regulate Qi; practice gentle movements like Tai Chi or Ba Duan Jin. Avoid: staying up late, prolonged sitting, or holding in anger. Be alert for: stubborn insomnia, skin rashes, digestive disorders. If minor discomfort appears, attend to it immediately; do not let it develop into chronic illness.
study: Studies are like 'those stubbornly lost'—cramming without proper method. The 'single finger' in the verse represents key guidance—a teacher's words or a classmate's casual insight. If missed, you remain stuck on the wrong path. Dun transforming into Pi indicates blocked thinking; exam luck is average. Avoid last-minute cramming or guessing; you may lose points on familiar topics. Advised: set aside pride, review fundamentals repeatedly, ask questions, and study in groups to open your mind. Avoid: closed-door hard study, blindly chasing progress, or changing methods at the last minute. If you are severely unbalanced in subjects, turn back to strengthen weaknesses—they may be the easiest to improve.
general: The essence of this sign lies in the two paths of delusion and awakening. Pi (Stagnation) of heaven and earth cannot be forced open by human effort—only when the light of the mind appears. Three concrete actions: first, when encountering a bottleneck, stop for three days and examine your own attachment. Second, following the 'single finger turning back,' actively seek advice from people or books you previously ignored. Third, avoid arguing over small matters; delay major decisions until the fog clears. In mindset, see this as a warning from heaven and earth—not a dead end, but a call to turn back. Calm impatience and stubbornness; when the mind is peaceful, the path naturally opens. Use this as a cultural perspective to inform your own judgment.

sign_text:
The moon rises in the east,
With clear light that can be cupped;
A thousand miles without a cloud,
The sea and sky are one emerald.
interpretation1: The sign verse depicts a moon rising over the sea—a symbol of a clear and smooth period of fortune. 'The moon rises in the east' points to a new opportunity emerging, like a new moon breaking dawn, gradually becoming full. It signifies that a new phase is unfolding, and the light of hope is within reach. 'Clear light that can be cupped' indicates that beauty and clarity are tangible—you can receive bright blessings without great effort. 'A thousand miles without a cloud' means obstacles have vanished, the situation is clear, and external interference has cleared away—you can act without hindrance. 'The sea and sky are one emerald' expands the realm to infinity—heaven and water merge, symbolizing harmony between person and time, suitable for both movement and stillness. The hexagram belongs to the Zhen Palace, with the original hexagram Xie (Release) transforming into Gui Mei (Returning Maiden). Xie represents thunder moving over water, the image of escaping difficulties—indicating that past entanglements have dissolved. Gui Mei (Returning Maiden) is thunder over the lake, symbolizing union and return—showing that after release, harmony and stability naturally come. Together, this sign is supremely favorable—like a bright moon emerging from the sea, clear light shining for a thousand miles. It indicates an open mind and harmonious affairs. However, a supremely favorable sign especially calls for cherishing the moment—do not become arrogant in good times. Hold to clarity and stillness to enjoy lasting brightness.
career: Career is like the moon rising over the sea, entering a clear brightness. 'The moon rises in the east' indicates new opportunities emerging—your position may see an upward turn, or you may take on an important project. Key times are when the moon phase changes or at dawn. 'Clear light that can be cupped' suggests results are achievable; effort readily yields returns. Take the initiative to take on core roles that showcase your talent and require communication and coordination—especially favorable for biddings, presentations, and external relations. 'A thousand miles without a cloud' symbolizes a clean workplace environment with orderly competition; petty people and rumors fade away. You can act boldly.
